Ayahs

قَدْ أَفْلَحَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١)
Certainly, the faithful have attained salvation (1)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى صَلَاتِهِمْ خَـٰشِعُونَ (٢)
—those who are humble in their prayers, (2)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ ٱللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ (٣)
avoid vain talk, (3)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِلزَّكَوٰةِ فَـٰعِلُونَ (٤)
carry out their [duty of] zakat, (4)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَـٰفِظُونَ (٥)
guard their private parts, (5)
إِلَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ (٦)
(except from their spouses or their slave women, for then they are not blameworthy; (6)
فَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَىٰ وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْعَادُونَ (٧)
but whoever seeks [anything] beyond that—it is they who are transgressors), (7)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَـٰنَـٰتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَٰعُونَ (٨)
and those who keep their trusts and covenants (8)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَوَٰتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ (٩)
and are watchful of their prayers. (9)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْوَٰرِثُونَ (١٠)
It is they who will be the inheritors, (10)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسَ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (١١)
who shall inherit paradise and will remain in it [forever]. (11)

The Virtue of Surah al-Mu’minun

In Islamic narrations transmitted to us from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, great virtue has been stated for this Surah.

In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: “Whoever recites Surah al-Mu’minun, the angels give him glad tidings on the Day of Resurrection of 'spirit and fragrance' (rest and provision) and that which delights his eye when the Angel of Death descends to take his soul.” [^1]

And we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: “Whoever recites Surah al-Mu’minun and continues to do so every Friday, Allah will seal the end of his life with felicity, and his station shall be in the Highest Paradise (al-Firdaws al-A’la) alongside the Prophets and the Messengers.” ^2

We deem it necessary to repeat this point: that mentioning the virtues of reciting the Surahs never means a recitation void of reflection, resolve, and action; for this Heavenly Book is a book of education, human-building, and practical programs. Truly, if someone aligns their creedal and practical programs with the content of this Surah—and even the few opening verses that describe the attributes of the believers—all those honors must become his portion.

Hence, it is narrated in some traditions from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that upon the revelation of the beginning of this Surah, He said: “Ten verses have been revealed to me; whoever establishes them (upholds them) shall enter Paradise!” [^3]

[^1] and ^2: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 7, p. 98. [^3]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 18, p. 2.

The expression "established" (aqama) instead of "recited" (qara’a) speaks to the same reality we mentioned above: that the primary goal is to implement the content of these verses into the context of life, not mere recitation.

Content of Surah al-Mu’minun

As is evident from its name, a significant portion of this Surah speaks of the chosen attributes of the believers. Subsequently, it presents discussions regarding belief and action that complement those attributes.

On the whole, the collective contents of this Surah can be divided into several sections:

Section One: Which begins from the verse “Successful indeed are the believers” and continues for several verses, explaining the attributes that are the source of the believers' success and salvation. We will see that these attributes are so calculated and comprehensive that they cover various aspects of individual and social life.

Since the essence of all of them is faith and Monotheism (Tawhid), the Second Section points to various signs of knowing God, and the horizons and internal signs of the Lord across the expanse of existence, enumerating examples of the wondrous system of creation in the heavens, the earth, and the creation of humans, animals, and plants.

To complete the practical aspects, the Third Section details the cautionary life stories of a group of great Prophets such as Nuh, Hud, Musa, and ‘Isa عليه السلام, recounting highlights of their lives.

In the Fourth Section, the discourse turns toward the arrogant, warning them with logical proofs and sometimes with sharp, crushing expressions, so that receptive hearts may awaken and find the path of return to Allah.

In the Fifth Section, concise discussions regarding the Resurrection (Ma’ad) are presented.

In the Sixth Section, it speaks of Allah’s sovereignty over the world of existence and the influence of His command throughout the entire universe.

Finally, in the Seventh Section, it once again mentions the Resurrection, the Reckoning, the Recompense, the reward of the righteous, and the punishment of the wicked, concluding the Surah by stating the purpose of human creation.

Thus, the content of this Surah is a collection of creedal and practical lessons, awakening matters, and a description of the believers' trajectory from beginning to end.

This Surah, as we stated at the beginning, was revealed in Makkah. However, some commentators have written that certain verses of this Surah were revealed in Madinah. The presence of the verse regarding Zakat led some to think that the entire Surah could not have been revealed in Makkah, because we know that Zakat was first legislated in Madinah, and following the revelation of the verse “Take alms from their wealth...” (Tawbah: 103), the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the Zakat collectors to go out and collect Zakat from the people.

However, it must be noted that "Zakat" has a broad meaning that includes both the obligatory and the recommended, and its meaning is not restricted to the obligatory Zakat. Thus, we read in narrations that prayer and Zakat have always been together. [^1] Furthermore, according to some scholars, Zakat was also obligatory in Makkah, but in a general and non-specific form—meaning everyone was duty-bound to give a portion of their wealth to the needy.

But in Madinah, where the Islamic government was formed, Zakat was placed under a precise program, thresholds (nisab) were set for it, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent collectors in every direction to receive Zakat from the people. ^2

[^1]: In a narration from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq, we read: “Allah ordained Zakat along with Prayer.” ^2: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 18, p. 2.

In the Name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful

Successful indeed are the believers (1) Those who are humble in their prayers (2) And those who turn away from vain talk (3) And those who are observant of Zakat (4)

And those who guard their private parts (5) Except from their wives or those whom their right hands possess, for then they are not blameworthy (6) But whoever seeks beyond that, then those are the transgressors (7) And those who are keepers of their trusts and their covenant (8) And those who maintain their prayers (9)

Those are the inheritors (10) Who will inherit Paradise (al-Firdaws); they will abide therein forever (11)

Commentary

The Outstanding Attributes of the Believers

As we said previously, the choice of the name "al-Mu’minun" (The Believers) for this Surah is due to its opening verses, which describe the characteristics of the believers in short, vivid, and content-rich phrases. Interestingly, it first points to the delightful and proud destiny of the believers—before stating their attributes—to ignite the flames of longing and love in hearts to reach this great honor.

It says: “Successful indeed are the believers,” and they have reached their ultimate goal in all dimensions (قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ).

"Aflaha" is derived from the root "falh" and "falah," which originally means to split or cut. Subsequently, it was applied to any type of victory and reaching a destination or happiness. In reality, victorious, successful, and happy individuals remove obstacles from their path, "split" their way toward the destination, and move forward.

Of course, "falah" (success/salvation) has a broad meaning that includes both material and spiritual victories, and in the case of the believers, both dimensions are intended.

Worldly success and salvation lie in a person living free, proud, honorable, and self-sufficient, and these matters are not possible except under the shadow of faith. The salvation of the Hereafter lies in living in the proximity of the Lord’s mercy, amidst eternal blessings, alongside worthy and pure friends, and in perfect honor and pride.

"Raghib" in al-Mufradat, while explaining this meaning, says: "Worldly success is summarized in three things: survival (baqa), self-sufficiency (ghina), and honor ('izz). The success of the Hereafter is in four things: survival without end, self-sufficiency without poverty, honor without humiliation, and knowledge without ignorance."

Then it proceeds to describe these attributes, and before anything else, it places its finger on prayer and says: “Those who are humble in their prayers” (الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي صَلاتِهِمْ خاشِعُونَ).

"Khashi'un" is from the root "khushu’," meaning a state of humility and physical and spiritual etiquette that arises in a person before a great personality or an important reality, and its effects appear in the body.

Here, the Quran does not count "establishing prayer" (performing prayer) as the sign of the believers, but rather counts "humility in prayer" as one of their characteristics. This indicates that their prayer is not a set of soulless words and movements void of meaning; rather, at the time of prayer, such a state of attention toward the Lord arises in them that they become detached from everything else and join Him. They become so immersed in the state of reflection, presence, and intimate supplication with the Lord that it affects every atom of their being. They see themselves as a tiny atom before an Infinite Being, and a drop before a boundless ocean.

Each moment of this prayer is a lesson for them in self-construction and human education, and a means for the purification of the soul and spirit.

In a hadith, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw a man playing with his beard while in prayer. He said: “As for him, if his heart were humble, his limbs would also be humble!” [^1] This points to the fact that humility is an internal state that manifests externally.

The great leaders of Islam had such humility in the state of prayer that they became completely estranged from everything other than Allah (ma siwa Allah), to the extent that we read in a hadith: "The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله used to sometimes look toward the sky during prayer, but when the above verse was revealed, He no longer raised His head and constantly looked toward the ground." ^2

The second attribute mentioned for the believers after the attribute of humility is: “And those who turn away from vain talk” (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ اللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ).

In fact, all their movements and the lines of their lives pursue a goal—a useful and constructive goal—because "laghw" (vain talk/action) means aimless actions without a useful result.

In reality, as some great commentators have said, "laghw" is any speech or action that lacks significant benefit. While we see some commentators have interpreted it as "falsehood," others as "all sins," others as "lies," others as "insults or responding to insults with insults," others as "music and idle play," and finally some as "polytheism"—all of these are instances of that comprehensive and general concept.

Of course, "laghw" does not only include useless words and deeds; rather, useless and baseless thoughts that distract a person from the remembrance of God and occupy them from reflecting on what is useful and constructive are all gathered within the concept of "laghw."

In reality, the believers are so constructed that they not only refrain from false thoughts, baseless words, and useless deeds, but in the Quran's expression, they are "mu'ridun" (those who turn away) from them.

In the next verse, it points to the third attribute of true believers, which has a social and financial aspect, saying: “And those who are observant of Zakat” (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ لِلزَّكاةِ فاعِلُونَ). [^1] Since this Surah—as we said before—is among the Surahs revealed in Makkah, and at that time the ruling of regular Zakat had not been revealed, commentators have various discussions regarding the interpretation of this verse.

[^1]: "Zakat" here has an infinitive (masdari) meaning, and for this reason, the expression "fa'ilun" (doers/performers) follows it. Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that Zakat carries its well-known meaning, i.e., a portion of wealth; in this case, "fa'ilun" would mean "mu'addun" (those who pay).

What seems more correct is that Zakat is not exclusively in the sense of obligatory Zakat; rather, recommended Zakat (alms) is abundant in Islamic law. What was revealed in Madinah was the obligatory Zakat, but recommended Zakat existed previously.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that Zakat existed as an obligatory ruling but without specific limits and boundaries in "Makkah"—meaning Muslims were duty-bound to pay a portion of their wealth to the needy—but after the formation of the Islamic government and the establishment of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal), Zakat was placed under a specific program with defined thresholds and amounts, and collectors were dispatched by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in every direction.

However, the interpretation cited by some commentators like Fakhr al-Razi, Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Raghib in al-Mufradat—that Zakat here means any kind of good deed or the purification (tazkiyah) of the soul and spirit—seems very unlikely. This is because in the Holy Quran, wherever prayer and Zakat are mentioned together, Zakat refers to financial spending (infaq), and using another meaning requires a clear context, which is not present here.

The fourth characteristic of the believers is the issue of complete chastity and modesty, and avoidance of any sexual pollution, stating: “And those who guard their private parts” [^1] (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ).

“Except from their wives or those whom their right hands possess, for then they are not blameworthy” (إِلَّا عَلى‌ أَزْواجِهِمْ أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ).

[^1]: "Furuj" is the plural of "farj," a metonym for the reproductive organs.

Since the sexual instinct is the most rebellious of human instincts, and self-restraint against it requires great piety, abstinence, and strong faith, the next verse emphasizes this issue once more, saying: “But whoever seeks beyond that, then those are the transgressors” (فَمَنِ ابْتَغى‌ وَراءَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ العادُونَ).

The expression "guarding" (muhafazat) of the private parts perhaps suggests that if there is no constant and persistent vigilance in this regard, there is a great fear of pollution.

The term "wives" includes both permanent and temporary wives, although some Sunni commentators have fallen into an error here which will be addressed in the "Key Points."

The expression “they are not blameworthy” (غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ) might point to a wrong way of thinking that emerged in deviant Christianity, where they consider any sexual intercourse as contrary to human dignity and view absolute abandonment of it as a virtue—to the extent that Catholic priests, as well as monks and nuns, live celibate lives for their entire lives, imagining any marriage to be contrary to this spiritual station! (Though this issue is mostly superficial, as in secret, a group of them choose ways to satisfy their sexual instinct, and the books of their own writers are full of stories written in this regard). In any case, it is impossible for Allah to create an instinct as part of the "Best System" (Nizam al-Ahsan) in humans and then completely prohibit it or consider it contrary to human status.

This point hardly needs reminding: that the lawfulness of wives is not inconsistent with certain exceptional cases, such as the state of menstruation and the like.

Similarly, the lawfulness of female slaves (concubines) is conditional upon numerous requirements mentioned in books of jurisprudence; it is not the case that every female slave is lawful to her owner. In fact, in many aspects and conditions, they share the same conditions as wives.

In the eighth verse under discussion, it points to the fifth and sixth outstanding attributes of the believers, saying: “And those who are keepers of their trusts and their covenant” (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَماناتِهِمْ وَ عَهْدِهِمْ راعُونَ).

Preserving and fulfilling trusts—in the broad sense of the word—as well as being bound to covenants and promises before the Creator and the creation, are among the prominent attributes of the believers.

In the broad concept of "trust" (amanah), the trusts of Allah and the Prophets, as well as the trusts of the people, are gathered. The various blessings of Allah are each a trust among His trusts. The true religion, the Heavenly Books, the instructions of the leaders of the path of truth, as well as wealth, children, posts, and positions—all are His trusts which the believers strive to preserve and fulfill. They guard them as long as they live, and upon leaving the world, they entrust them to their capable future generations, training such a generation to guard them.

The proof for the generality of the concept of trust here, in addition to the breadth and absolute nature of the word, is the numerous narrations regarding the interpretation of "trust." Sometimes trust is interpreted as the "Imamate of the Infallible Imams," which each Imam entrusts to the Imam after him, [^1] and sometimes as absolute authority (wilayah) and governance.

Interestingly, "Zurarah," who is one of the great students of Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, says: "The meaning of the phrase 'that you render back trusts to their owners' (Surah an-Nisa: 58) is that you should entrust authority and governance to those worthy of it!" ^2

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 380.

This shows that governance is among the most important divine deposits that must be entrusted to those worthy of it.

Similarly, the proof for the generality of "covenant" (ahd) is the expressions found in other verses of the Quran, including: “And fulfill the covenant of Allah when you have taken a covenant” (an-Nahl: 91).

It is noteworthy that in some verses of the Quran, the expression used is "fulfilling the trust" or "not betraying the trust," whereas in the verse under discussion, the expression is "observing (ra'un) the trust," which includes both fulfillment and perfect protection and vigilance over it.

Therefore, if negligence in repairing something held in trust causes damage or danger, the trustee is duty-bound to strive for its repair as well (thus, three actions are necessary: fulfillment, preservation, and maintenance).

In any case, it is certain that being bound to commitments and preserving and fulfilling trusts are among the most important foundations of the human social system, and without them, chaos will emerge throughout society. For this reason, even individuals and nations that do not have divine or religious beliefs consider themselves duty-bound to carry out these two programs—at least in general social matters—to remain safe from the social chaos resulting from betrayal of covenants and trusts.

Regarding the importance of trust, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 432 onwards (under verse 58 of Surah an-Nisa), and in Volume 7, page 136 (under verse 27 of Surah al-Anfal). Regarding the fulfillment of covenants, see Volume 4, page 242 (under verse 1 of Surah al-Ma'idah) and Volume 11, page 382 (under verse 91 of Surah an-Nahl).

Finally, in the ninth verse, it states the last characteristic of the believers, which is the maintenance of prayers: “And those who maintain their prayers” (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى‌ صَلَواتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ).

Interestingly, it counted "humility in prayer" as the first characteristic of the believers and "maintenance of prayer" as their last attribute. It begins with prayer and ends with prayer, because prayer is the most important connection between the creation and the Creator.

Prayer is the most superior school of high education. Prayer is the means for the awakening of the soul and spirit, and an insurance for the human being against sins. In short, whenever prayer is performed with its etiquettes, it will be a reliable ground for all goodness and virtues.

It is also necessary to remind that the first verse and the last verse point to two different matters. For this reason, in the first verse, "prayer" (salat) is in the singular form, and in the last verse, it is in the plural form (salawat). The first refers to the issue of humility and specific internal attention, which is the soul of prayer and affects all limbs. The second refers to the issue of the etiquettes and conditions of prayer in terms of time, period, and place, as well as the number of prayers, advising the prayer-performers and true believers to be mindful of all these etiquettes and conditions in all prayers.

Regarding the importance of prayer, we have discussed it in detail in various volumes of this Tafsir: Refer to Volume 9, page 267 (under verse 114 of Surah Hud); Volume 4, page 104 (under verse 103 of Surah an-Nisa); And Volume 13 (under verse 14 of Surah Ta-Ha).

After mentioning these distinguished attributes, it states the final result as follows: “Those are the inheritors” (أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْوارِثُونَ).

The same inheritors who inherit Firdaws and the Highest Paradise and will abide therein eternally (الَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ الْفِرْدَوْسَ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ).

"Firdaws" originally—according to some—is a Roman word, some say it is Arabic, and some say its origin is Persian, meaning "garden," or a specific garden in which all divine blessings and favors are gathered. Therefore, it can be named as "the Highest Paradise" (the best and most superior of the gardens of Heaven).

The expression "inheriting" might point to the fact that the believers reach it without toil, just like an inheritance for which a person has not labored. It is true that attaining the high stations of Paradise requires much effort, struggle, purity, and self-construction, but that immense reward in exchange for these meager deeds is so great that it is as if the person reached it without toil.

It is also necessary to note that in a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated: “There is not one of you except that he has two abodes: an abode in Paradise and an abode in the Fire. If he dies and enters the Fire, the people of Paradise inherit his abode.” The expression "inheritance" in the verse under discussion might also point to this.

Some commentators have also considered it not unlikely that the expression "inheritance" here refers to the end of the believers' affair, like a legacy that reaches the heir at the end of the process.

In any case, this high stage of Paradise, according to the apparent meaning of the above verses, is specific to the believers who possess the aforementioned attributes. Accordingly, other inhabitants of Paradise are in lower stages.

Key Points

1- Why was the past tense "aflaha" chosen?

The choice of the past tense "aflaha" (has succeeded) regarding the salvation of the believers is for the greatest possible emphasis—meaning their success is so certain that it is as if it has already been realized. The mention of the word "qad" before it is another emphasis on the subject.

Expressions such as "khashi'un," "mu'ridun," "ra'un," and "yuhafizun" (in the form of active participles or present/future tense verbs) all prove that the programs of true believers in these distinguished attributes are not temporary or limited, but rather continuous and permanent.

2- Permanent and Temporary Wives

From the above verses, it is understood that only two groups of women are lawful for men: first, wives, and second, female slaves (with specific conditions). For this reason, this verse has been cited in many instances in books of jurisprudence in discussions of marriage.

A group of Sunni commentators and jurists have sought to bring a proof from this verse to negate temporary marriage (mut'ah), saying that it too is in the ruling of adultery!

However, considering the reality that "temporary marriage" (mut'ah) was certainly lawful during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and not a single Muslim denies this—except that some say it was at the beginning of Islam and many of the Companions practiced it, then it was abrogated, and others say Umar ibn al-Khattab prohibited it.

Given these realities, the implication of the words of this group of Sunni scholars would be that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—God forbid—permitted adultery, at least for a time, and this is impossible.

Furthermore (pay attention), mut'ah, contrary to the imagination of this group, is a type of marriage—a temporary marriage possessing most of the conditions of permanent marriage. Therefore, it is definitely included in the phrase “except from their wives.” For this reason, when reciting the formula (sighah) for temporary marriage, the same marriage formulas as permanent marriage (ankahtu and zawwajtu) are used with the condition of a time limit, and this is the best proof of it being a marriage.

Regarding temporary marriage, the proofs for its legitimacy in Islam, the non-abrogation of this ruling, as well as its social philosophy and responses to various objections, we have discussed in detail in Volume 3, page 335 onwards (under verse 24 of Surah an-Nisa).

3- Humility is the Soul of Prayer

If we consider bowing (ruku’), prostration (sujud), recitation (qira’ah), and glorification (tasbih) as the body of the prayer, then the presence of heart and internal attention to the reality of the prayer and the One with whom we are conversing is the soul of the prayer.

Humility (khushu’) is in reality nothing but the presence of heart coupled with modesty, etiquette, and respect. Thus, it becomes clear that the believers do not look at prayer merely as a soulless frame; rather, all their attention is toward the inner essence and reality of the prayer.

There are many who have a great desire for presence of heart and humility in their prayers, but no matter how they strive, they do not find the success for it.

To acquire humility and presence of heart in prayer and other acts of worship, we strictly recommend the following matters:

  1. Acquiring such knowledge (ma’rifah) that makes the world seem small in one's eyes and God seem great, so that no worldly affair can attract one's attention or distract one from God during intimate supplication with the Beloved.
  2. Attention to scattered and various tasks usually prevents concentration. To the extent that a person succeeds in reducing distracting and scattered preoccupations, they have helped the presence of heart in their worship.
  3. The choice of the place and location for prayer and other acts of worship also has an effect on this matter. For this reason, it is disliked (makruh) to pray in front of objects and things that occupy the mind, as well as in front of open doors, places where people pass, or in front of mirrors and pictures. Therefore, the simpler the places of worship for Muslims and the more they are free from glitter and formalities, the better it is, as it aids the presence of heart.
  4. Avoiding sin is also an effective factor, because sin distances the heart from God and reduces the presence of heart.
  5. Familiarity with the meaning of the prayer and the philosophy of its actions and litanies is another effective factor.
  6. Performing the recommended (mustahabb) acts of prayer and its specific etiquettes, whether in the preliminaries or in the prayer itself, also provides effective help to this matter.
  7. Beyond all this, this task, like any other task, requires vigilance, practice, continuity, and persistence. It often happens that at the beginning, a person is able to concentrate for only a short moment during the entire prayer, but by continuing this work and persisting in it, they gain such strength of soul that they can absolutely close the gates of their thought to anything other than the Beloved during prayer! (Pay attention).

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن سُلَـٰلَةٍ مِّن طِينٍ (١٢)
Certainly We created man from an extract of clay. (12)
ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ نُطْفَةً فِى قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ (١٣)
Then We made him a drop of [seminal] fluid [lodged] in a secure abode. (13)
ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا ٱلنُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا ٱلْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا ٱلْمُضْغَةَ عِظَـٰمًا فَكَسَوْنَا ٱلْعِظَـٰمَ لَحْمًا ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَـٰهُ خَلْقًا ءَاخَرَ ۚ فَتَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقِينَ (١٤)
Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass. Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue. Then We created the fleshy tissue as bones. Then We clothed the bones with flesh. Then We produced him as [yet] another creature. So blessed is Allah, the best of creators! (14)
ثُمَّ إِنَّكُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ (١٥)
Then indeed you die after that. (15)
ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ تُبْعَثُونَ (١٦)
Then you will indeed be raised up on the Day of Resurrection. (16)

Commentary

The Stages of Embryonic Development in the Womb

The mention of the characteristics of true believers and the unparalleled reward Allah grants them in the preceding verses awakens a longing in hearts to join their ranks. But through what means? And by which path?

The verses under discussion, along with a portion of the upcoming verses, demonstrate the fundamental ways to acquire faith and gnosis. First, they take the hand of man and lead him to explore the secrets within and to "journey through the world of the self" (sayr fi al-anfus). In the verses that follow, they direct his attention to the external world and the wondrous beings of the universe, engaging in the "horizonal journey" (sayr afaqi).

First, He says: "And indeed, We created man from an extract of clay" (وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ مِنْ سُلَالَةٍ مِنْ طِينٍ) [1]. Yes, this is the first step: that man, with all that greatness, all those talents and merits—this most noble of creatures and the superior being of the world—originates from insignificant dust. It is the very dust that is a proverb for low value, and this is the ultimate manifestation of His power: that from such simple materials, He created such an ingenious being.

In the next verse, He adds: "Then We placed him as a drop (sperm-drop) in a safe lodging" (ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرَارٍ مَكِينٍ).

In reality, the first verse refers to the beginning of the existence of all human beings, including Adam and his children, as all return to the earth and have risen from clay. However, the second verse draws attention to the continuity of Adam's progeny through the combination of male and female gametes and their placement in the sanctuary of the womb.

In fact, this discussion is similar to the expression found in verses 7 and 8 of Surah As-Sajdah: "He began the creation of man from clay, then He made his progeny from an extract of a lowly fluid" (وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسَانِ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلَالَةٍ مِنْ مَاءٍ مَهِينٍ).

Describing the womb as a "safe lodging" (qararin makin) refers to the specific position of the womb in the human body. It is situated in the most protected part of the body, completely shielded from every side.

[1]: "Sulahah" (on the weight of usarah) means something taken from another; it is effectively the extract, essence, and choice part of it (Majma' al-Bayan regarding the verse under discussion).

The spinal column and ribs on one side, the powerful pelvic bone on another, the multiple layers of the abdomen on a third, and the protection provided by the arms on a fourth—all are evidence of this safe and secure lodging.

Then, He refers to the wondrous and astonishing stages of the sperm-drop's journey in the mother's womb and the various forms of creation that appear one after another in that safe lodging, far from human reach, saying: "Then We made the sperm-drop into a clinging clot, then We made the clinging clot into a lump of flesh, then We made the lump of flesh into bones, and then We clothed the bones with flesh" (ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظَامًا فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظَامَ لَحْمًا).

These four distinct stages, which together with the stage of being a sperm-drop constitute five stages, each possess a wondrous world of their own, full of marvels that have been precisely studied in modern embryology and about which books have been written. Yet, on the day the Quran spoke of these various stages of human embryonic creation and its wonders, there was no trace of this science and knowledge.

At the end of the verse, He refers to the final stage, which is in fact the most important stage of human creation, with a concise and meaningful expression, saying: "Then We developed him into another creation" (ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْنَاهُ خَلْقًا آخَر).

"So blessed is Allah, the Best of creators" (فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ).

Praise be to this peerless manifestation of power, which in the darkness of the womb paints such an ingenious image with all these wonders and marvels upon a drop of water.

Praise be to that Knowledge and Wisdom that creates all this talent, merit, and worthiness in such an insignificant being. Praise be to Him and His unique creation.

Additionally, it should be noted that "Khaliq" (Creator) is derived from the root khalq, and khalq originally means "measuring." When a piece of leather is measured for cutting, the Arabs use the word khalq for it. Since the issue of measuring is more important than anything else in creation, this word khalq has been used for it.

The expression "the Best of creators" (Ahsan al-Khaliqin) raises the question: Is there any creator other than Allah?! Some commentators have offered various justifications for the verse, while there is no need for such justifications. The word khalq in the sense of measuring and craftsmanship is also applicable to those other than Allah. However, of course, Allah's creation differs from the creation of others in various ways:

  • Allah creates the matter and the form of things, whereas if a human wants to create something, he can only give a new form to it by using existing materials in this world—for example, building a house from construction materials, or inventing a car or a factory from iron and steel.

  • On the other hand, Allah's creation is unlimited, and He is the Creator of all things: "Allah is the Creator of all things" (Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 16). Meanwhile, man can only innovate very limited beings, and sometimes they are accompanied by various weaknesses and defects that must be perfected during the process. But the creation and innovation of the Lord is free from any flaw or defect.

  • Thirdly, where man finds the ability for such a thing, that too is by the permission and command of Allah; without His permission, not even a leaf on a tree moves in the universe. As we read regarding Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him) in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 110: "And when you created from clay the likeness of a bird by My permission" (وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِي).

The verse following the issue of Monotheism and the recognition of the Originator transitions beautifully and subtly to the issue of the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), saying: This human, with all his wonders, does not live forever. A time comes when this wondrous structure collapses, and "Then indeed, after that, you will surely die" (ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ بَعْدَ ذَلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ).

But so that the notion does not arise that everything ends with human death (implying that this creation with all its glory and greatness was a futile matter for these few days of life), He immediately adds: "Then indeed, on the Day of Resurrection, you will be resurrected" (certainly at a higher level and in a vast and expanded world) (ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ تُبْعَثُونَ).

Key Points

1- Proving the Origin and the Resurrection with One Argument

It is interesting that in the above verses, to prove the existence of Allah and His power and greatness, the same argument is used as in Surah Al-Hajj to prove the Resurrection—namely, the issue of the various stages of human creation in the embryonic world. Coincidentally, at the end of these very verses under discussion, as we saw, a transition was made to the issue of the Resurrection [1].

[1]: At the beginning of Surah Al-Hajj, under verses 5 to 7, we discussed the proofs of the Resurrection, including through embryology (refer to the beginning of this 14th volume).

Yes, on one hand, one can recognize the greatness of Allah from the wonders of human creation in the hidden place of the womb, which takes on a new shape and design every day—as if a group of master painters and a group of skilled craftsmen and innovators are sitting beside this drop of water, working on it day and night, passing this insignificant particle through various stages and gateways of life with extraordinary delicacy in a very short time.

If we could fully film the stages of embryonic growth and development and pass them before our eyes, we would then understand what wonders are hidden in this work.

Although the extraordinary progress of embryology in our era and the increasing research of scientists and their experiments on this matter have clarified many issues, when a person is confronted with the results of this research, he involuntarily murmurs the phrase: "So blessed is Allah, the Best of creators" (فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ).

On the other hand, these successive creations that take on a new face every day, and fundamentally the emergence of a complete human being from a small drop of water, demonstrate Allah's power over the issue of the Resurrection and the return of man to a renewed life. In this way, by stating one reason, two goals are achieved, and with one gesture, two tasks are performed [1].

2- The Final Stage of Human Development in the Womb

It is interesting that in the five stages mentioned for human creation in the above verse, the term khalq (creation) is used everywhere, but when it reaches the final stage, it uses the term insha' (development/origination).

Insha', as linguists have stated, means "to bring something into existence accompanied by its nurturing." This expression shows that the final stage is completely different from the previous stages (the stage of the sperm-drop, the clinging clot, the lump of flesh, and the flesh and bone). It is an important stage that the Quran mentions concisely, saying only: "Then We developed him into another creation," and immediately following it says: "So blessed is Allah, the Best of creators."

What is this stage that is so worthy of importance? This is the very stage where the embryo enters the phase of human life, acquires sensation and movement, and begins to stir—which in Islamic narrations is referred to as the stage of "the breathing of the spirit" (nafkh al-ruh).

It is here that man, with a great leap, leaves behind vegetative and plant life and steps into the world of animals, and higher still, into the world of humans. The distance between it and the previous stage is so great that describing it with the phrase "then We created" (thumma khalaqna) was not sufficient, and therefore He said: "Then We developed him" (thumma ansha'nahu).

It is here that man acquires a special structure that distinguishes him from the entire universe, gives him the worthiness of Allah's vicegerency on earth, and the lot of the Trust—which the mountains and heavens could not bear—is cast in his name.

In reality, it is right here that the "macrocosm" ('alam al-kabir) with all its wonders is enfolded and wrapped within this "small body" (jurm al-saghir), and truly it is worthy of "So blessed is Allah, the Best of creators."

3- A Garment of Flesh over the Frame of Bones!

The author of the commentary Fi Zilal (In the Shade of the Quran), under the verse discussed here, quotes a remarkable point: after the embryo passes the stages of "clinging clot" ('alaqah) and "lump of flesh" (mudghah), all its cells turn into bone cells, and after that, muscles and flesh gradually cover them. Therefore, the phrase "We clothed the bones with flesh" (kasawna al-'izama lahma) is a scientific miracle that unveils this matter which was not clear to anyone at that time. For the Quran does not say: "We turned the lump of flesh into bone and flesh," but rather says: "We turned the lump of flesh into bones and clothed the bones with a garment of flesh," indicating that the lump of flesh first turns into bone and after that, flesh covers it.

4- A Resistant Garment for the Bones!

Fundamentally, the fact that He describes the muscles as a "garment" (libas) itself speaks to the reality that if this garment were not over the bones, the human form would be very ugly and unsightly (just like the skeletons that we have all seen, or at least seen pictures of).

Beyond this, a garment is a protector of the body; the muscles likewise protect the bones. If they were not there, the impacts delivered to the body would constantly damage or break the bones. Furthermore, the work that clothing does in protecting man from heat and cold, the flesh does in maintaining the bones, which are the main pillars of the body. All of these indicate the precision of the Quran in its expressions.

[1]: Regarding the stages of the embryo and the masterpiece of creation in this field, we have also discussed in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 316 (under the verse: "He it is Who shapes you in the wombs as He wills," Surah Al-Imran, verse 6).

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرَآئِقَ وَمَا كُنَّا عَنِ ٱلْخَلْقِ غَـٰفِلِينَ (١٧)
Certainly We created above you seven levels and We have not been oblivious of the creation. (17)
وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءًۢ بِقَدَرٍ فَأَسْكَنَّـٰهُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰ ذَهَابٍۭ بِهِۦ لَقَـٰدِرُونَ (١٨)
We sent down water from the sky in a measured manner, and We lodged it within the ground, and We are indeed able to take it away. (18)
فَأَنشَأْنَا لَكُم بِهِۦ جَنَّـٰتٍ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَـٰبٍ لَّكُمْ فِيهَا فَوَٰكِهُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (١٩)
Then with it We produced for you gardens of date palms and vines. There are abundant fruits in them for you, and you eat from them, (19)
وَشَجَرَةً تَخْرُجُ مِن طُورِ سَيْنَآءَ تَنۢبُتُ بِٱلدُّهْنِ وَصِبْغٍ لِّلْـَٔاكِلِينَ (٢٠)
and a tree that grows on Mount Sinai, which produces oil and a seasoning for those who eat. (20)
وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ لَعِبْرَةً ۖ نُّسْقِيكُم مِّمَّا فِى بُطُونِهَا وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٢١)
There is indeed a moral for you in the cattle: We give you to drink of that which is in their bellies, and you have many uses in them, and you eat some of them, (21)
وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ (٢٢)
and you are carried on them and on ships. (22)

Commentary

More Signs of Monotheism

We have stated that after mentioning the attributes of the believers, the Quran addresses the means of acquiring faith. In the preceding verses, as we observed, it spoke of the "inner signs" (ayat al-anfusi) and the marks of the Lord's greatness within our own existence. Now, it turns to the external world and the "outer signs" (ayat al-afaqi), reflecting the grandeur of creation in the heavens and the earth:

First, It says: "And We have indeed created above you seven ways" (وَ لَقَدْ خَلَقْنا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرائِقَ).

Taraiq (طرائق) is the plural of tariqah (طريقة), meaning "way/path" or "layer/stratum." According to the first meaning, the verse implies: "We created seven paths above your heads." These paths might be the routes of the angels' travel, or perhaps the orbits of the celestial bodies.

According to the second meaning, the concept is: "We created seven layers (seven heavens) above you."

Regarding the seven heavens, we have discussed this extensively before. What should be noted here as a brief reference is that if we take the number seven to signify "multiplicity," the meaning is that above you are numerous worlds and countless spheres, stars, and planets.

The term "layer" (tabaqah) should never evoke the Ptolemaic spheres, which were imagined to be layered like the skins of an onion, nor should it be thought that the Quran relies on that incorrect hypothesis. Rather, "ways and layers" refer to worlds situated at various distances from us, each being "above" the other relative to us—some further and some closer.

If we take the number sab'a (seven) as a specific count, the meaning is that besides this world you see (the collection of fixed stars, planets, and galaxies), there are six other worlds above it that the hand of human science and knowledge has not yet reached.

Furthermore, if we carefully examine the map of the solar system and the positioning of the various planets, another interpretation for this verse can be found: of the planets orbiting the Sun, two (Mercury and Venus) have orbits below the Earth's orbit, while six other planets have orbits outside the Earth's orbit, resembling layers positioned one above the other. When we add the orbit of the Moon, which also revolves around the Earth, the number of seven orbits (paths) or seven layers is completed (reflect on this). [^1]

[^1]: For further explanation regarding the seven heavens, refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 29 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Since the multiplicity of worlds and their paths might give rise to the misconception of whether this vastness and grandeur of the universe would cause the Creator to become heedless of them, the end of the verse immediately states: "And We have never been, nor shall We ever be, heedless of [Our] creation" (وَ ما كُنَّا عَنِ الْخَلْقِ غافِلِينَ).

The emphasis on the term "creation" (khalq) here indicates that the matter of creation itself is proof of the Creator's knowledge and His attention toward them. How could the "Creator" be heedless of His "creature"?!

There is also a possibility in the interpretation of the verse that it means: "We have placed many paths for the coming and going of angels above you, and We are not heedless of your state, and Our angels are also witnesses and observers of your deeds."

The next verse points to another manifestation of Divine power, which is considered among the blessings of the heavens and the earth—namely, rain—saying: "And We sent down water from the sky in a measured amount" (وَ أَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً بِقَدَرٍ). Neither so much as to drown the lands, nor so little that the thirsty ones in the world of plants and animals remain unquenched.

Indeed, moving from the "heavens" to the "earth," one of the most important Divine gifts is water, which is the source of life for all living beings.

Then, it addresses a more important matter in this regard—the storage of water in underground reservoirs—saying: "Then We caused it to settle in the earth, and indeed We are surely able to take it away" (فَأَسْكَنَّاهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ إِنَّا عَلى‌ ذَهابٍ بِهِ لَقادِرُونَ).

We know that the Earth's crust consists of two completely different layers: a permeable layer and an impermeable layer. If the entire crust were permeable, rainwater would immediately sink into the depths of the earth, and after a long, continuous rain, everywhere would become dry and not a drop of water would be found! And if the entire crust were an impermeable layer like clay, all rainwater would remain on the surface, becoming contaminated and foul-smelling, making the Earth's surface unbearable for humans; thus, this water, which is the source of life, would become the cause of death.

However, the Great and Gracious Allah made the upper layer permeable and the lower layer impermeable so that water sinks into the earth, is harnessed at the impermeable zone, and is stored, to be used later through springs, wells, and qanats (aqueducts), without rotting, causing nuisance, or becoming contaminated. [^1] This sweet water that we draw today from a deep well and find new life by drinking may be from raindrops that descended from the clouds thousands of years ago and were stored in the depths of the earth for today, without spoiling.

In any case, He who created man for life and made water his most important source of life, created very significant reservoirs for storing this vital substance before him and stored the waters therein! Of course, a portion of the reservoirs of this vital substance is atop the mountains (in the form of snow and ice), which sometimes melts and flows annually, and sometimes remains on a mountain peak for hundreds or thousands of years until the day the command to descend is given, and due to changes in atmospheric temperature, it flows down toward the plains and valleys to quench the thirsty lands.

[^1]: It should be noted that the passage of contaminated water through the permeable layers of the earth often results in its purification!

However, considering the word "in" (fi) in "in the earth" (fi al-ard), it appears that the verse refers to underground water sources rather than those above ground.

In the next verse, following the mention of the blessed gift of rain, it refers to the products that grow from it, saying: "Then We produced for you therewith gardens of date-palms and grapes, wherein is much fruit for you, and of them you eat" (فَأَنْشَأْنا لَكُمْ بِهِ جَنَّاتٍ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَ أَعْنابٍ لَكُمْ فِيها فَواكِهُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ). Dates and grapes are not their only products; rather, these are the two primary and highly valuable products, otherwise, various other types of fruits are found therein.

The phrase "and of them you eat" (وَ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ) might indicate that the yield of these blessed gardens is not limited to their fruits; rather, eating is only a part of their utility.

These gardens (including palm groves) have many other uses for human life: from their leaves, mats and sometimes clothing are made; from their wood, houses are built; from the roots, leaves, and fruits of some of these trees, medicinal substances are extracted; and also from many of them, fodder for livestock is obtained, and their wood is used for fuel.

Fakhr al-Razi, in his commentary, has also suggested the possibility that "and of them you eat" means that your life and sustenance are managed through these gardens, just as we say in Persian: "So-and-so eats bread from such-and-such a business" (meaning his livelihood is derived from it). [^1] It is also noteworthy that in the above verses, the origin of human life is considered "water" (semen), and the origin of plant life is "rainwater." In fact, these two prominent examples of life both originate from water. Indeed, Allah's law is a single and expansive law everywhere.

[^1]: According to the first interpretation, min is "partitive" (tab'idiyyah), and according to the second, it is "causal/originative" (nashwiyyah).

Then, it points to another blessed tree nourished by this same rainwater, saying: In addition to these gardens of palms, grapes, and other fruits, "And a tree that springs forth from Mount Sinai, which produces oil and a relish [^1] for those who eat" (وَ شَجَرَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ طُورِ سَيْناءَ تَنْبُتُ بِدهْنِ وَ صِبْغٍ لِلْآكِلِينَ). Regarding what is meant by "Tur Sina" (Mount Sinai), commentators have offered two main possibilities:

First, it refers to the famous "Mount Tur" located in the Sinai Peninsula. If we see that the Quran describes the olive tree here as a tree that grows from Mount Tur, it is because when the Arabs of Hijaz passed through the dry deserts of that region and headed north, the first area where they encountered fruitful olive trees was the Tur region in the south of the Sinai Peninsula. A look at a geographical map clarifies this point well.

The other possibility is that "Tur Sina" is descriptive, meaning "blessed mountain," "tree-covered mountain," or "beautiful mountain" (since Tur means mountain and Sina means blessed, beautiful, and wooded).

The word sibgh (صبغ) originally means "color" or "dye," but since a person usually colors their bread with the relish they eat, all types of relishes/stews are called sibgh. In any case, the word sibgh might refer to the olive oil itself which they ate with bread, or various relishes obtained from other trees.

A question arises here: why, among all types of fruits, is emphasis placed specifically on these three: dates, grapes, and olives?

In response, one should note that according to the scientific research of nutritionists, few fruits exist that are as beneficial and effective for the human body as these three.

"Olive oil" has great value for the body's metabolism; its caloric value is very high, it is a close friend to the human liver, a reliever of kidney ailments, gallstones, and renal colic, a strengthener of nerves, and ultimately an elixir of health.

Regarding "dates," so much has been described that it cannot fit in this brief space: the abundant sugar in dates is among the healthiest sugars, and according to many nutritionists, dates are one of the factors in preventing cancer. Scientists have discovered thirteen vital substances and five types of vitamins in dates, making them an exceptionally valuable food source.

As for "grapes," according to some scientists, they are a natural pharmacy. In terms of properties, they are like mother's milk and produce twice as much heat in the body as meat; they purify the blood, eliminate toxins from the body, and the various vitamins present in them provide strength and energy to humans. [^1]

[^1]: For further explanation regarding these three life-giving fruits, refer to Volume 11, page 172 onwards (under verse 11 of Surah An-Nahl).

After explaining a portion of the Lord's blessings in the plant kingdom nourished by rainwater, it turns to a significant part of His blessings and gifts in the animal kingdom, saying: "And indeed, for you in the livestock is a great lesson" (وَ إِنَّ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَنْعامِ لَعِبْرَةً). [^1] Then it explains this lesson as follows: "We give you to drink of what is in their bellies" (نُسْقِيكُمْ مِمَّا فِي بُطُونِها). Yes, wholesome milk—this nourishing and complete food—We bring forth from within these animals, from between blood and the like, so that you may know how Allah has the power to bring forth such a pure and pleasant drink from among such apparently contaminated things.

Then it adds: the lesson-bearing matters and blessings of animals are not limited to milk; rather, "and for you in them are many benefits, and of them you eat" (وَ لَكُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ).

In addition to meat, which in moderation constitutes a major part of the nutritional materials required by the body, you prepare various clothes and durable tents from their leather, various garments, coverings, and carpets from their wool, medicinal substances from some parts of their bodies, and even fertilizers for trees and crops from their waste.

Beyond all this, you use livestock as swift mounts on land and ships for the seas; "and upon them and upon the ships you are carried" (وَ عَلَيْها وَ عَلَى الْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ) to reach your intended destinations. [^1] All these effects, properties, and benefits in these animals are truly a cause for reflection; they both acquaint man with the Creator of all these blessings and arouse a sense of gratitude within him. [^2] The only question remaining here is how livestock and ships are placed in the same category? With one point, the answer becomes clear: because man needs transport across the entire earth, alongside land transport, He mentions sea transport—namely ships. In fact, this expression is similar to what is stated in verse 70 of Surah Al-Isra regarding the gifts to the Children of Adam:

"And We carried them on land and sea" (وَ حَمَلْناهُمْ فِي الْبرِّ وَ الْبَحْرِ).

[^1]: The indefiniteness of 'ibrah (lesson) here indicates its greatness. [^1]: In Volume 11, page 344 (under verse 80 of Surah An-Nahl), we have discussed the uses derived from animals in detail. [^2]: Regarding the importance of ships and the various properties of matter that allow for the utilization of ships, we have discussed in Volume 11, page 181 (under verse 14 of Surah An-Nahl) and also in this volume (Volume 14) under verse 65 of Surah Al-Hajj.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٢٣)
Certainly We sent Noah to his people, and he said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (23)
فَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُرِيدُ أَن يَتَفَضَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَنزَلَ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً مَّا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِىٓ ءَابَآئِنَا ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٢٤)
But the elite of the faithless from among his people said, ‘This is just a human being like you, who seeks to dominate you. Had Allah wished, He would have sent down angels. We never heard of such a thing among our forefathers. (24)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌۢ بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌ فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ بِهِۦ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (٢٥)
He is just a man possessed by madness. So bear with him for a while.’ (25)

Commentary

The Logic of the Blind-Hearted Arrogant

Since the previous verses discussed Monotheism (Tawhid), the knowledge of Allah, and the signs of His greatness in the world of creation, the verses under discussion and the subsequent ones express the same subject through the tongues of the great Prophets and within the context of their history.

It begins first with Noah عليه السلام, the first of the Ulul 'Azm (Arch-Prophets) and the herald of Monotheism, saying: "We sent Noah to his people, and he said to them: 'O my people! Worship Allah, the One and Only God, for you have no deity other than Him'" (وَ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً إِلى‌ قَوْمِهِ فَقالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ‌).

"Will you not then fear [Him] despite this clear explanation regarding the worship of idols?" (أَ فَلا تَتَّقُونَ‌).

"But the assembly of the wealthy, arrogant aristocrats—who outwardly fill the eyes but are empty within—from among his people said: 'This man is nothing but a human being like yourselves, with the exception that a sense of superiority has been stirred within him and he wishes to dominate and rule over you!'" (فَقالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ ما هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَتَفَضَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ‌).

In this way, they considered his being human as his first flaw, and subsequently accused him of being a power-seeker, claiming that his words about God, Monotheism, and religion were all a conspiracy to achieve this goal! Then they added: "If Allah had willed to send a messenger, He would surely have sent down angels for this purpose" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ لَأَنْزَلَ مَلائِكَةً).

To complete this hollow argument, they said: "We have never heard of such a thing among our forefathers of old—that a human should claim prophethood and consider himself a representative of God!" (ما سَمِعْنا بِهذا فِي آبائِنَا الْأَوَّلِينَ‌).

However, these baseless words had no effect on the spirit of this great Prophet, and Noah عليه السلام continued his invitation. Since there was no sign of superiority-seeking or power-lust in his conduct, they accused him of another charge, which was "madness and insanity"—a charge that all divine Prophets and true leaders have been accused of throughout history. They said:

"He is only a man afflicted with a kind of madness; so wait concerning him for a while until his death arrives or he is cured of this madness!" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ فَتَرَبَّصُوا بِهِ حَتَّى حِينٍ‌).

It is interesting that in their accusation against this great Prophet, they used the expression "بِهِ جِنَّةٌ" (possessed by a kind of madness) to cover up the reality that the life of this Prophet and his words were all the best signs of his intellect and knowledge. In fact, they wanted to say that all of this is true, but madness has various forms and faces, some of which manifest as intellect!! The phrase "فَتَرَبَّصُوا بِهِ حَتَّى حِينٍ‌" (wait concerning him for a while) might refer to waiting for Noah's death, for which the opponents were counting the minutes so they could be at ease; or it might be an emphasis on the attribution of madness to him, meaning: wait until he recovers from this illness! [1] In any case, in their speech, they attributed three types of hollow and contradictory accusations to Noah, taking each as a reason to deny his mission.

First, that fundamentally, the claim of a messenger coming from among humans is a lie! Such a thing had no precedent; if God had willed, He should have sent angels.

Second, that he is a power-seeking man who has used this claim as a means to reach

[1]: Some have also said this phrase indicates that he should be imprisoned for a while, and others have said it means to leave him alone for now until later, but these two interpretations do not seem very correct.

this goal.

Third, that he does not have a sound mind, and what he says stems from this!

And since the response to these baseless and scattered objections and accusations was clear and has appeared in other verses of the Quran, the Quran says nothing further here in this regard.

For on one hand, it is certain that the leader of humans must be of their own kind so that he is familiar with human needs, pains, and issues; furthermore, Prophets have always been of the human race.

On the other hand, it is clearly evident from the lives of the Prophets that the issue of brotherhood, humility, and the rejection of any kind of power-seeking were among their most prominent traits, and their intellect, intelligence, and wisdom were apparent even to their enemies, who would confess to it within their own statements.

Ayahs

قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ (٢٦)
He said, ‘My Lord! Help me, for they impugn me.’ (26)
فَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ أَنِ ٱصْنَعِ ٱلْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا وَفَارَ ٱلتَّنُّورُ ۙ فَٱسْلُكْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَوْلُ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تُخَـٰطِبْنِى فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ (٢٧)
So We revealed to him: ‘Build the ark before Our eyes and by Our revelation. When Our edict comes and the oven gushes [a stream of water], bring into it a pair of every kind [of animal], and your family, except those of them against whom the decree has gone beforehand, and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoers: they shall indeed be drowned.’ (27)
فَإِذَا ٱسْتَوَيْتَ أَنتَ وَمَن مَّعَكَ عَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ فَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى نَجَّىٰنَا مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٨)
‘When you, and those who are with you, are settled in the ark, say, ‘‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has delivered us from the wrongdoing lot.’’ (28)
وَقُل رَّبِّ أَنزِلْنِى مُنزَلًا مُّبَارَكًا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْمُنزِلِينَ (٢٩)
And say, ‘‘My Lord! Land me with a blessed landing, for You are the best of those who bring ashore.’’ ’ (29)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ وَإِن كُنَّا لَمُبْتَلِينَ (٣٠)
There are indeed signs in this; and indeed, We have been testing. (30)

Commentary

The End of a Rebellious People's Life

In the previous verses, we read some of the unjust accusations that the enemies of Nuh (Noah) hurled at him. However, it is clearly understood from other verses of the Quran that the harassment from this rebellious people was not limited to these matters; rather, they pressured and tormented him by every possible means. Nuh exerted his utmost effort and endeavor to guide them and save them from the clutches of polytheism and disbelief. When he became despaired of his efforts and saw that none but a small group had believed, he sought help from God, as we read in the first verse under discussion:

"He said: My Lord! Help me because they have denied me" (قالَ رَبِّ انْصُرْنِي بِما كَذَّبُونِ‌) [1]

At this point, the command of the Lord arrived, and the preliminaries for the salvation of Nuh and his few companions, and the destruction of the stubborn polytheists, were prepared.

[1]: The "Bā’" in "بِما كَذَّبُونِ‌" may be the "Bā’ of Causality" (Sababiyyah) or "Bā’ of Recompense" (Muqābalah), and the "Mā" in "بِما كَذَّبُونِ‌" may be "Mā al-Masdariyyah" or "Mā al-Mawsūlah," which would result in slightly different meanings; however, this difference is not significant (reflect on this).

"So We revealed to him: Build the ship under Our eyes and according to Our command" (فَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِ أَنِ اصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا وَ وَحْيِنا).

The expression "under Our eyes" (بِأَعْيُنِنا) indicates that your effort and struggle in this path are in Our presence and under the cover of Our protection; therefore, continue your path with a calm and reassured mind and fear nothing.

Furthermore, the expression "Our revelation" (وَحْيِنا) shows that Nuh learned the method and manner of shipbuilding through Divine revelation; otherwise, such a thing—according to historical records—had no precedent until then. For this reason, Nuh built the ship so perfectly suited to its purpose and objective that it lacked nothing! Then it continues: "When Our command comes, and its sign is that water shall gush forth from the oven, know that the time of the storm has approached; immediately take on board a pair (male and female) of every kind of animal" (فَإِذا جاءَ أَمْرُنا وَ فارَ التَّنُّورُ فَاسْلُكْ فِيها مِنْ كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ‌).

"And embark your family and your believing friends, except those against whom the word [of destruction] has already gone forth" (referring to Nuh's wife and one of his sons) (وَ أَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَنْ سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ الْقَوْلُ مِنْهُمْ‌).

And He adds further: "And do not speak to Me concerning those who have done wrong (who wronged both themselves and others), for they are all to be drowned, and there is no room for discussion" (وَ لا تُخاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِنَّهُمْ مُغْرَقُونَ‌).

Of course, this warning was because Nuh might have been influenced by human emotions or the bond between father and son and might have interceded for them again, while they were no longer worthy of intercession.

In the next verse, He says: "And when you and those with you are settled on the ship, praise Allah for this great blessing and say: All praise is due to Allah, Who has delivered us from the wrongdoing people" (فَإِذَا اسْتَوَيْتَ أَنْتَ وَ مَنْ مَعَكَ عَلَى الْفُلْكِ فَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي نَجَّانا مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

And after praising and glorifying Allah for His first great blessing—namely, salvation from the clutches of the oppressors—make this request from His threshold: "And say: My Lord! Cause me to land at a blessed landing-place, for You are the best of those who bring to land" (وَ قُلْ رَبِّ أَنْزِلْنِي مُنْزَلًا مُبارَكاً وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْمُنْزِلِينَ‌). The word "Munzal" may be a "Noun of Place" (Ism Makān), meaning: after the storm ends, land our ship in a land that possesses abundant blessings so that we may continue our lives with peace of mind.

It may also be a "Mimic Infinitive" (Masdar Mīmī), meaning: land us in a worthy manner, because after the storm ended and the ship settled on the ground, many dangers threatened the passengers—the lack of a suitable place to live, shortage of food, and various diseases. Nuh asks Allah to land him on the earth in a healthy and proper manner.

Finally, the last verse under discussion refers to the entirety of this story, saying: "Indeed, in this [story of Nuh and his victory over the oppressors and the punishment of this rebellious people in the severest way] are signs for those of intellect and thought!" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ‌).

"And We surely put [everyone] to the test" (وَ إِنْ كُنَّا لَمُبْتَلِينَ‌).

This sentence may refer to the fact that We tested the people of Nuh repeatedly, and when they failed the tests, We destroyed them.

It may also refer to the fact that We test all human beings in every age and time, and what was mentioned in the above verses was not exclusive to the people of Nuh's era; rather, in all ages and centuries, tests take place in various forms. In these tests, those who are thorns in the path of human perfection are removed from the way so that humanity may continue its evolutionary journey.

It is interesting that in the above verses, the focus is only on the matter of building the ship and Nuh and his companions embarking upon it, while nothing is mentioned about what ultimately happened to the sinners. This is because, with the Divine promise "they are to be drowned" (إِنَّهُمْ مُغْرَقُونَ‌), it becomes certain that such a fate overtook them, for His promise is never broken.

It is also necessary to mention that regarding the people of Nuh, their struggle against this great Prophet, their painful fate, the story of building the ship, the gushing of water from the oven, the occurrence of the storm, and the drowning of Nuh's son, there is much to say. We have provided a large portion of this in detail in Surah Hud, Volume 9, from page 68 to page 125, and God willing, another part will come in the commentary of Surah Nuh.

Ayahs

ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا ءَاخَرِينَ (٣١)
Then after them We brought forth another generation, (31)
فَأَرْسَلْنَا فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٣٢)
and We sent them an apostle from among themselves, saying, ‘Worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (32)
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَتْرَفْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يَأْكُلُ مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ مِنْهُ وَيَشْرَبُ مِمَّا تَشْرَبُونَ (٣٣)
Said the elite of his people, who were faithless and who denied the encounter of the Hereafter and whom We had given affluence in the life of the world: ‘This is just a human being like yourselves: he eats what you eat, and drinks what you drink. (33)
وَلَئِنْ أَطَعْتُم بَشَرًا مِّثْلَكُمْ إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ (٣٤)
If you obey a human being like yourselves, you will indeed be losers. (34)
أَيَعِدُكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ إِذَا مِتُّمْ وَكُنتُمْ تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَنَّكُم مُّخْرَجُونَ (٣٥)
Does he promise you that when you have died and become bones and dust you will indeed be raised [from the dead]? (35)
۞ هَيْهَاتَ هَيْهَاتَ لِمَا تُوعَدُونَ (٣٦)
Far-fetched, far-fetched is what you are promised! (36)
إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ (٣٧)
There is nothing but the life of this world: we live and die, and we will not be resurrected. (37)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا وَمَا نَحْنُ لَهُۥ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (٣٨)
He is just a man who has fabricated a lie against Allah, and we will not believe in him.’ (38)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ (٣٩)
He said, ‘My Lord! Help me, for they impugn me.’ (39)
قَالَ عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ لَّيُصْبِحُنَّ نَـٰدِمِينَ (٤٠)
Said He, ‘In a little while they will become regretful.’ (40)
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ بِٱلْحَقِّ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ غُثَآءً ۚ فَبُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤١)
So the Cry seized them justly and We turned them into a scum. So away with the wrongdoing lot! (41)

Commentary

The Tragic Fate of Another People (The People of Thamud)

These verses discuss other nations that came into power after Nuh عليه السلام, describing their logic—which harmonized with the logic of previous disbelievers—as well as their painful fate, thereby completing the discussions mentioned in the preceding verses.

First, it states: "Then We produced after them another generation" (ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْناً آخَرِينَ).

The word qarn (generation) is derived from the root iqtiran, meaning "closeness" or "conjunction." Therefore, a population living in the same era is called a qarn, and sometimes their time period is also referred to as a qarn. Measuring the duration of a qarn as thirty or a hundred years is merely conventional and depends on the traditions of various peoples.

Since humanity cannot exist without a divine leader, Allah sent a great Prophet to them to spread the call of monotheism and the religion of truth and justice, as the next verse says: "And We sent among them a messenger from themselves, [saying], 'Worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him'" (فَأَرْسَلْنا فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِنْهُمْ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ).

This was the very foundation of the call of all Prophets; it was the cry of monotheism (Tawhid), which is the bedrock of all individual and social reforms.

Then, for further emphasis, this divine Messenger would say to them: "Will you not then fear [Him]?" (أَ فَلا تَتَّقُونَ) — meaning, despite this explicit call, will you still not refrain from polytheism and idolatry?

Regarding which nation this was and what their Prophet's name was, commentators have offered two possibilities based on similar verses in the Quran:

First, that it refers to the "People of Thamud," who lived in a land north of the Hijaz, and the great divine Prophet Salih عليه السلام was appointed to guide them. They disbelieved and chose the path of rebellion, and were ultimately destroyed by a heavenly blast (a deadly thunderbolt).

The evidence for this interpretation is the punishment of the "blast" (sayhah) mentioned at the end of these verses, which is also explicitly mentioned regarding the "People of Salih" in Surah Hud, verse 67.

The second possibility is that it refers to the "People of ‘Ad," whose Prophet was Hud عليه السلام. In some Quranic verses, their story follows immediately after the story of Nuh, which serves as a contextual clue for this interpretation [1]. However, considering that the punishment of this people, according to verses 6 and 7 of Surah Al-Haqqah, was a violent wind that battered them for seven nights and eight days, it becomes clear that the first interpretation is more accurate.

[1]: Refer to Surah Hud verse 50, Surah Al-A'raf verse 65, and Surah Ash-Shu'ara verse 123.

In any case, let us see what the reaction of this rebellious people was to the monotheistic call of this great Prophet. The Quran says in the next verse: "And the eminent among his people who disbelieved and denied the meeting of the Hereafter while We had given them luxury in the worldly life said, 'This is not but a man like yourselves. He eats of that from which you eat and drinks of what you drink'" (وَ قالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ كَذَّبُوا بِلِقاءِ الْآخِرَةِ وَ أَتْرَفْناهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا ما هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يَأْكُلُ مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ مِنْهُ وَ يَشْرَبُ مِمَّا تَشْرَبُونَ).

Indeed, the group living in luxury and opulence—referred to by the Quran as the mala’ (those whose outward appearance fills the eye but whose inner being is void of the light of truth)—rose in opposition because they saw the call of this great Prophet as contrary to their whims and a hindrance to their illegitimate interests, arrogance, and baseless superiority. Due to these luxuries, they had drifted far from God and denied the abode of the Hereafter, using the exact same logic as the rebels among the people of Nuh.

They took the fact that these divine leaders were human and ate and drank like other people as a reason to negate their prophethood, whereas this was actually a confirmation of the mission of these great men—that they rose from among the masses so they could perfectly understand their pains and needs.

Then they said to one another: "And if you should obey a man like yourselves, indeed, you would then be losers" (وَ لَئِنْ أَطَعْتُمْ بَشَراً مِثْلَكُمْ إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً لَخاسِرُونَ).

These blind-hearted people failed to notice that they themselves expected people to follow them in these satanic whisperings and join them in opposing this Prophet. Yet, they considered following someone who receives help from the source of revelation and whose heart is illuminated by the light of the Lord's knowledge to be a flaw and contrary to human liberty and freedom!

Then they proceeded to deny the Resurrection—the acceptance of which has always been a barrier in the path of the self-willed and the hedonists—and said: "Does he promise you that when you are dead and have become dust and bones, you will be brought forth [once more]?" (أَ يَعِدُكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ إِذا مِتُّمْ وَ كُنْتُمْ تراباً وَ عِظاماً أَنَّكُمْ مُخْرَجُونَ).

"Far, far is that which you are promised" (baseless and hollow promises!) (هَيْهاتَ هَيْهاتَ لِما تُوعَدُونَ). Is it even possible that a human who has died, turned to dust, and whose particles have scattered in every direction could return to life? Such a thing is impossible, impossible!!

Then they emphasized their denial of the Resurrection further by saying: "It is not but our worldly life; we die and we live, and we will not be resurrected" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا نَمُوتُ وَ نَحْيا وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ). Meaning, there is nothing but this worldly life; one group dies and another generation takes their place, and after death, there is no news!

Finally, as a summary of the accusations they leveled against their Prophet, they said: "He is not but a man who has invented a lie about Allah, and we will not believe him" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً وَ ما نَحْنُ لَهُ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ).

He has no mission from God, his promises of resurrection are not true, nor are his other programs; therefore, a sane person will not believe in him.

When their pride and rebellion exceeded all bounds, and they tore away all veils of modesty, reaching the peak of shamelessness in denying the mission, miracles, and the soul-building call of their Prophet—in short, after the proof was completed against them—this great divine Prophet turned to the threshold of God and "said, 'My Lord, support me because they have denied me'" (قالَ رَبِّ انْصُرْنِي بِما كَذَّبُونِ). They said whatever they could and leveled whatever accusations they wished; now, You help me.

"And it was said by the Lord: 'In a little while they will surely become regretful'" and they will taste the fruit of the bitter tree they have planted (قالَ عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ لَيُصْبِحُنَّ نادِمِينَ). But they will regret it at a time when it is of no use and the way back is closed.

And so it happened: "So the blast seized them in truth" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّيْحَةُ بِالْحَقِّ). A deadly thunderbolt with a terrifying and horrific sound descended, shaking everything, crushing and destroying, piling their lifeless bodies upon one another. It was so swift and crushing that they did not even have the power to flee their homes and were buried within them, as the Quran says at the end of these verses:

"And We made them as plant stubble" (فَجَعَلْناهُمْ غُثاءً) — like crushed debris atop a flood.

"So away with the wrongdoing people!" (فَبُعْداً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ).

Key Points

1. The Lavish Life and Its Sinister Effect
