In the above verses, a connection is seen between itraf (an aristocratic life of luxury) and "disbelief and denial of the meeting with the Lord." Truly, it is so, because owners of such a lifestyle usually desire unconditional freedom for every kind of pleasure-seeking and animalistic enjoyment. It is quite clear that accepting divine oversight and the great court of Resurrection is a major obstacle in this path; it both disturbs their peace of conscience and opens the people's tongues against them.

Therefore, such individuals suddenly cast off the yoke of servitude to the Lord and take the path of denying the Origin and the Return. As we read in the verses above, they say: life is this and nothing more, and there is no other news! One must seize the moment and enjoy these four days of life; one must pluck a flower from every meadow and seek pleasure from every means! In this way, they justify their wrongdoings and the ugliness of their actions.

Furthermore, providing such a glittering life is usually impossible without usurping the rights of others and practicing oppression. Unless they deny the mission of the Prophets and the Resurrection, this path will not be smooth for them. This is why we see the majority of those who possess such a life turning their backs on everything and looking at everything with an eye of contempt and denial.

These blind-hearted wretches and captives in the clutches of whims and desires leave the shadow of obedience and grace of the Lord, but they place the yoke of servitude to whims, desires, and lust around their necks, becoming slaves to other slaves. They have debased thoughts, polluted souls, and black, dark hearts. The prospect of their lives might be attractive to some from afar, but it is terrifying up close, because the restlessness resulting from sin and the fear of the decline of blessings and death keeps their minds in constant anxiety.

2. "Dust" and "Bones"

Turab means dust and ‘izam means bones. Usually, the human body first turns into decayed bones and then into dust. Therefore, the question remains: why is "dust" mentioned before "bones" in the verse above?

This expression might refer to two different parts of the human body: first, the flesh falls away and becomes dust, while the bones remain for years afterward before they eventually decay and disappear.

Another noteworthy possibility is that "dust" refers to very ancient ancestors who have all become dust, and "bones" refers to fathers whose decayed bones still remain [1].

[1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verses discussed.

3. What is "Ghutha’"?

We read in the verses above that the "People of Thamud" became like ghutha’ due to the heavenly blast. Ghutha’ originally refers to dried plants that are scattered in a very disordered manner atop a flood. It also refers to the foam that appears on a boiling pot.

Likening their lifeless bodies to ghutha’ points to their ultimate weakness, helplessness, brokenness, and worthlessness. For the debris atop a flood is the most worthless and lightest of things; it has no will of its own, and after the flood passes and subsides, no trace of it remains.

Regarding the heavenly blast (sayhah), we provided a detailed explanation in Volume 9, page 164 (under verse 67 of Surah Hud). Of course, this punishment was not exclusive to the people of Thamud; rather, several sinful nations were destroyed by this same divine punishment, the details of which we explained there.

4. A Universal Fate

Interestingly, in the final sentence of the verses under discussion, the issue is taken out of its specific form and expressed as a general and universal law:

"Away with the wrongdoing people from the mercy of God." This is, in fact, the final conclusion of all these verses: that what was said in this account—regarding the denial and rejection of divine verses, the denial of the Hereafter and Resurrection, and their painful result—is not limited to a specific population or group, but includes all oppressors throughout history.

Ayahs

ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قُرُونًا ءَاخَرِينَ (٤٢)
Then after them We brought forth other generations. (42)
مَا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ (٤٣)
No nation can advance its time nor can it defer it. (43)
ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا تَتْرَا ۖ كُلَّ مَا جَآءَ أُمَّةً رَّسُولُهَا كَذَّبُوهُ ۚ فَأَتْبَعْنَا بَعْضَهُم بَعْضًا وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ ۚ فَبُعْدًا لِّقَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٤٤)
Then We sent Our apostles successively. Whenever there came to a nation its apostle, they impugned him, so We made them follow one another [to extinction] and We turned them into folktales. So away with the faithless lot! (44)

Commentary

Rebellious Nations Were Destroyed One After Another

Following the conclusion of the story of the people of Thamud, the Quran, in the verses under discussion, refers to other nations that came into power after them and before Musa عليه السلام, stating: "Then We produced after them other generations" (ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ قُرُوناً آخَرِينَ).

This is because it is the law and Sunnah of the Great Allah that He does not cut off His grace; if a group becomes an obstacle in the path of the evolution of mankind, He sets them aside and continues to lead this caravan on its journey.

However, these various nations and tribes each had a specific time and term, and "No nation can precede its term, nor can they delay it" (ما تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَها وَ ما يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ). Rather, when the definitive decree for the end of their life was issued, they would perish—not a moment sooner, nor later.

"Ajal" means the lifespan and duration of something, and sometimes "ajal" is also used to refer to the end point or deadline, such as when we say: the "ajal" of such a debt is such a time (meaning its maturity date).

Of course, as we have mentioned before, "ajal" is of two types: "definite" (hatmi) and "conditional or suspended" (mu'allaq).

The definite term is the time of the final end of the life of a person, a nation, or a thing, in which no change is possible. However, the conditional or suspended term is a time that may increase or decrease with the changing of conditions and obstacles. We have already discussed this sufficiently [^1]. In any case, it is clear that the above verse refers to the "definite term."

The next verse points to the fact that the invitation of the Prophets throughout history was never

[^1]: For more explanation on this matter, please study Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 148 onwards (under verse 2 of Surah Al-An'am).

interrupted; it says: "Then We sent Our messengers in succession" (ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلَنا تَتْرا).

"Tatra" is derived from the root "watr," meaning to come one after another. "Tawatur al-akhbar" refers to reports that arrive one after another, from the collection of which a person gains certainty. This root is originally taken from "watr," meaning "bowstring," because the string is attached to the bow and positioned behind it, bringing the two ends of the bow together (from the perspective of word structure, "tatra" was originally "watra," where the 'waw' was changed to 'ta').

In any case, these heavenly teachers came and went one after another, but the rebellious nations remained persistent in their disbelief and denial, such that "Every time there came to a nation its messenger, they denied him" (كُلَّ ما جاءَ أُمَّةً رَسُولُها كَذَّبُوهُ).

When this disbelief and denial exceeded the limit and the proof was sufficiently established, "We made some of them follow others [to destruction] and We wiped them off the face of the earth" (فأَتْبَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ بَعْضاً).

They were destroyed in such a way that only talk of them remained among the people; yes, "We made them [mere] stories" (وَ جَعَلْناهُمْ أَحادِيثَ).

This indicates that sometimes a nation becomes extinct, but some individuals or significant traces of them remain scattered here and there. However, sometimes they are so utterly destroyed that nothing remains of them except a name on the pages of history or in the conversations of people, and these rebellious and tyrannical nations were of the second category [^1].

[^1]: "Ahadith" is the plural of "hadith," and its interpretation is as we mentioned above. However, some have suggested it might be the plural of "uhduthah," which means strange news that people talk about with one another (Tafsir Fakhr Razi, under the verse in question).

And at the end of the verse, like the preceding verses, it says: "So away with a people who do not believe" (فَبُعْداً لِقَوْمٍ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

Yes, these painful destinies were the result of their lack of faith, and for this reason, it is not exclusive to them. Any faithless, rebellious, and oppressive group will inevitably be caught in such a fate; they will be so destroyed that only a name remains of them in the pages of history and conversations.

They were not only far from the mercy of Allah in this world, but they are also far from Divine mercy in the Hereafter, because the expression of the verse is absolute and encompasses everything.

Ayahs

ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَأَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٤٥)
Then We sent Moses and Aaron, his brother, with Our signs and a manifest authority (45)
إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا عَالِينَ (٤٦)
to Pharaoh and his elites; but they acted arrogantly and they were a tyrannical lot. (46)
فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَنُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ مِثْلِنَا وَقَوْمُهُمَا لَنَا عَـٰبِدُونَ (٤٧)
They said, ‘Shall we believe two humans like ourselves, while their people are our slaves?’ (47)
فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا فَكَانُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْلَكِينَ (٤٨)
So they impugned the two of them, whereat they were among those who were destroyed. (48)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (٤٩)
Certainly We gave Moses the Book so that they might be guided, (49)

Commentary

The Uprising of Moses and the Destruction of the Pharaohs Arrives

Up to this point, the discussion has been about the nations that rose and fell before Moses عليه السلام, the Ulul-Azm Prophet of the Lord. However, in the verses under discussion, a very brief reference is made to the uprising of Moses and Aaron against the Pharaohs and the ultimate fate of this arrogant people, stating:

"Then We sent Moses and his brother Aaron with Our signs and a clear authority" (ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنا مُوسى‌ وَ أَخاهُ هارُونَ بِآياتِنا وَ سُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

Regarding what is meant by "signs" (ayat) and "clear authority" (sultan mubin), and the difference between the two, commentators have offered various interpretations:

  1. Some have said that "signs" refers to the miracles Allah granted to Moses son of Imran (the nine signs), while "clear authority" refers to the powerful logic and crushing arguments of Moses عليه السلام against the Pharaohs.

  2. Others suggest that "signs" refers to all of Moses' miracles, while "clear authority" refers to certain major miracles like the "Staff" and the "White Hand," as these possessed a unique quality that led to Moses' clear dominance and victory over the Pharaohs.

  3. Some have also suggested the possibility that "signs" refers to the verses of the Torah and the explanation of laws and the like, while "clear authority" refers to the miracles of Moses.

However, considering the usage of "clear authority" (sultan mubin) in the Holy Quran, the first interpretation seems closer to the truth, as in several instances in the Quran, the word sultan or sultan mubin is used to mean clear evidence and logic. [1]

[1]: In Surah An-Naml, verse 21, we read: "I will surely punish him with a severe punishment or slaughter him unless he brings me a clear authority (sultan mubin)." And in verse 23 of Surah An-Najm, we read: "They are not but [mere] names you have named them—you and your forefathers—for which Allah has sent down no authority (sultan)."

Yes, We sent Moses and his brother Aaron with these signs and clear authority "to Pharaoh and his arrogant, aristocratic inner circle" (إِلى‌ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ مَلَائِهِ‌).

Why does it only mention the mala' (the affluent and arrogant aristocratic group) and not say they were sent to all the people of Egypt? This is an indication that these people were the root of all corruption; if they were reformed, the rest would be easy. Furthermore, they were the leaders and officials of the country, and no country will be reformed unless its leaders are reformed.

"But Pharaoh and his inner circle acted arrogantly" and did not submit to the signs of truth and the clear authority (فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا).

"And fundamentally, they were a people who were haughty and sought dominance" (وَ كانُوا قَوْماً عالِينَ‌).

The difference between the phrase "they acted arrogantly" (istakbaru) and the phrase "they were a haughty people" (kanu qawman 'alin) might be that the first refers to their arrogance in the face of Moses' call, while the second indicates that arrogance was always part of their program and the fabric of their thought and soul.

There is also the possibility that the first refers to their act of arrogance, and the second refers to the fact that they possessed superior power and lifestyle, which was the major factor behind their arrogance.

One of the clear signs of their haughtiness was that "they said: 'Shall we believe in two humans like ourselves while their people (the Children of Israel) are our servants and slaves?'" (فَقالُوا أَ نُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ مِثْلِنا وَ قَوْمُهُما لَنا عابِدُونَ‌) [1]

[1]: A human is called bashar because his basharah (skin) is bare, unlike animals which are usually covered by a specific natural clothing. In fact, because animals lack the power to provide the necessities of life, Allah has provided them naturally, whereas for humans, it has been entrusted to their intelligence and reason.

Not only should we not submit to them, but they must always serve us! They accused the prophets of seeking superiority and dominance, while they themselves were the worst seekers of dominance, and the traces of this vile trait are clearly visible in this statement of theirs.

In any case, with these hollow arguments, they rose in opposition to the truth "and they denied Moses and Aaron, and eventually they were all destroyed" and their kingdom and government vanished (فَكَذَّبُوهُما فَكانُوا مِنَ الْمُهْلَكِينَ‌). Thus, the primary enemies of the Children of Israel, who were blocking the path of Moses and Aaron's call, were eliminated, and the era of divine education and training for the Children of Israel arrived.

It was at this stage that Allah revealed the Torah to Moses and invited the Children of Israel to implement its programs, as the final verse under discussion states:

"We gave Moses the Scripture so that the Children of Israel might be guided by its light" (وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ‌).

It is noteworthy that in the previous verses, during the stage of struggle against the Pharaohs, both Moses and his brother Aaron were mentioned, and all pronouns were in the dual form. However, here, where the revelation of the Heavenly Book is discussed, only Moses is mentioned, because he was the Ulul-Azm Prophet and the possessor of the Book and the new Sharia. Additionally, at the time of the Torah's revelation, he was on Mount Tur while his brother Aaron remained among the people of the Children of Israel. [1]

[1]: Regarding Moses عليه السلام, the nature of his appointment, and the details of his struggles with Pharaoh and the Pharaohs, we had detailed discussions in Volume 6 (under verses 103 to 162 of Surah Al-A'raf) and in Volume 13 (under verses 8 to 97 of Surah Ta-Ha).

Ayah

وَجَعَلْنَا ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُۥٓ ءَايَةً وَءَاوَيْنَـٰهُمَآ إِلَىٰ رَبْوَةٍ ذَاتِ قَرَارٍ وَمَعِينٍ (٥٠)
and We made the son of Mary and his mother a sign, and sheltered them in a level highland with flowing water. (50)

Commentary

Another Sign Among the Signs of Allah

In the final stage of recounting the stories of the Prophets, a brief and concise reference is made to Prophet Jesus عليه السلام and his mother Maryam, stating: "And We made the son of Maryam and his mother a sign [of Our greatness and power]" (وَ جَعَلْنَا ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَ أُمَّهُ آيَةً).

The use of the expression "the son of Maryam" instead of "Jesus" is to draw attention to the fact that he was born solely from a mother, without the intervention of a father, by the command of the Lord; and this birth itself was among the great signs of the Lord's power.

Since this exceptional birth relates both to Jesus and to his mother Maryam, He considers both of them as a single sign (Ayah), because these two (the birth of a child without the intervention of a father and a mother becoming pregnant without contact with a man) were in reality one truth with two different attributions.

Then, referring to a portion of the great blessings and favors He bestowed upon this mother and son, He says: "And We sheltered them on high ground, a place of rest and security with flowing water" (وَ آوَيْناهُما إِلى‌ رَبْوَةٍ ذاتِ قَرارٍ وَ مَعِينٍ‌).

"Rabwah" is derived from the root "raba", meaning increase and growth, and here it refers to an elevated land or plateau.

"Ma'in" is derived from the root "ma'n" (rhyming with shan), meaning the flow of water; therefore, ma'un ma'in means flowing water. Some have also considered it to be from the root "ayn", meaning water that is visible and can be seen with the eye [^1]. In any case, this sentence is a veiled reference to the safe, secure, and blessed place that Allah provided for this mother and son, so they would be safe from the evil of enemies and could fulfill their duties with peace of mind.

But as for where this location was, there is much discussion among the commentators.

Some consider it to be Nazareth (one of the cities of the Levant), the birthplace of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, because at the time of his birth, a group of enemies who had briefly learned of his birth and his future sought to destroy him, but Allah preserved him in that safe and bountiful place.

Others consider it a reference to the land of Egypt, as Jesus and his mother Maryam took refuge in Egypt for a period of their lives to escape the clutches of enemies.

Others have interpreted it as the land of "Damascus," and some as the land of "Ramla" (a city northeast of Jerusalem), because the Messiah and his mother spent a portion of their lives in each of these regions.

The possibility also exists that the above sentence refers to the birthplace of Jesus عليه السلام in the wilderness of Jerusalem, a place where Allah provided safety for this mother and son, caused sweet water to flow therein, and granted her sustenance from a withered date palm.

In any case, the verse is evidence of Allah's continuous and permanent support for His messengers and those who supported them. It shows that even if all the blades in the world move to sever a vein, they will have no power unless Allah wills it, and their loneliness or fewness of companions will never be the cause of their defeat.

[^1]: In the first case, the letter Meem is part of the root and follows the pattern of "Fa'eel", and in the second case, the Meem is an augmentative prefix and follows the pattern of "Maf'ool" (like Mabee').

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرُّسُلُ كُلُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَٱعْمَلُوا۟ صَـٰلِحًا ۖ إِنِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ (٥١)
O apostles! Eat of the good things and act righteously. Indeed I know best what you do. (51)
وَإِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَأَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمْ فَٱتَّقُونِ (٥٢)
Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so be wary of Me. (52)
فَتَقَطَّعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُم بَيْنَهُمْ زُبُرًا ۖ كُلُّ حِزْبٍۭ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ (٥٣)
But they fragmented their religion among themselves, each party boasting about what it had. (53)
فَذَرْهُمْ فِى غَمْرَتِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (٥٤)
So leave them in their stupor for a while. (54)

Commentary

All of You Are One Ummah

In the preceding verses, the discussion focused on the history of the Prophets and their nations. In the first verse under discussion, Allah addresses them all, saying: "O Messengers! Eat of the good things (Tayyibat) and perform righteous deeds; indeed, I am well-aware of what you do" (يَا أَيُّهَا الرُّسُلُ كُلُوا مِنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَاعْمَلُوا صَالِحًا إِنِّي بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ).

The difference between you and other human beings is not that you lack human qualities like the need for nourishment; rather, the difference is that you have accepted even nourishment as a means for spiritual perfection. For this reason, your program is to eat from the Tayyibat (pure and good things), whereas people who have made eating their ultimate goal are in no way bound by this program; they pursue whatever satisfies their animalistic desires, whether it be vile (khabith) or pure (tayyib).

Considering that the type of nourishment certainly affects a person's psyche, and that different foods have different moral effects, the connection between these two phrases becomes clear: "Eat of the good things" and "perform righteous deeds."

In Islamic narrations, we also read that consuming forbidden (haram) food prevents the acceptance of a person's supplications. A famous Hadith narrated from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله bears witness to this claim: A man said to him, "I wish for my prayers to be answered." He replied: "Purify your food, and do not let anything forbidden enter your stomach!" [^1] [^2] It is also necessary to note that the phrase "Indeed, I am well-aware of what you do" (إِنِّي بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ) is an independent motivation for performing righteous deeds. For when a person knows that someone is constantly observing his actions—someone from whom nothing is hidden and who keeps a precise account of his deeds—this awareness undoubtedly influences the improvement of his conduct.

Furthermore, the expressions in the above verse influence human actions by awakening a sense of gratitude toward Allah for the pure blessings He has bestowed. Thus, this verse utilizes three avenues to encourage righteous deeds: the effect of pure food on the clarity of the heart, the stimulation of the sense of gratitude, and the reminder that Allah is the Witness and Observer of man's actions.

As for the word Tayyib, as we have mentioned before, it means anything clean and pure, as opposed to Khabith (unclean/vile). Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Tayyib originally refers to anything pleasurable, whether it pleases the human senses or the soul and spirit; however, in religious law, it refers to that which is pure and lawful (halal)."

In any case, many discussions in the Quran revolve around Tayyib and Tayyibat: - It commands the Prophets to eat only from Tayyib food. - It addresses not only the Prophets but all believers: "O you who believe! Eat of the good things (Tayyibat) wherewith We have provided you" (Al-Baqarah: 172). - Only deeds and words that are Tayyib and pure find their way to His proximity: "To Him ascends the good word (Al-Kalim al-Tayyib), and the righteous deed lifts it" (Fatir: 10). - One of the great honors Allah has bestowed upon humanity, cited as a sign of his superiority over other creatures, is the use of Tayyibat: "And We have certainly honored the children of Adam and carried them on the land and sea and provided for them of the good things (Tayyibat) and preferred them over much of what We have created, with [definite] preference" (Al-Isra: 70).

In a short and profound Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, this reality is also expressed: "O people! Indeed, Allah is Pure (Tayyib) and He accepts nothing except that which is pure (Tayyib)." [^1]

Then, the next verse invites all Prophets and their followers to monotheism and piety, saying: "Indeed, this Ummah of yours is one Ummah" (and the differences between you and your Prophets are never a reason for duality or multiplicity) (وَإِنَّ هَذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً). "And I am your Lord, so fear Me" (وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاتَّقُونِ).

In this way, the above verse calls for the unity and oneness of human society and the elimination of all forms of discrimination and separation. Just as He is the One Lord, humans are also one Ummah. For this reason, they must follow a single program, just as their Prophets invited them to a single religion whose principles and foundations were the same everywhere: monotheism and the recognition of Truth, attention to the Hereafter and the evolutionary life of man, the use of Tayyibat, the performance of righteous deeds, and the defense of justice and human principles.

Some commentators interpret "Ummah" here to mean religion (din) or creed, rather than a population or community. However, the plural pronoun in the phrase "I am your Lord" (أَنَا رَبُّكُمْ) serves as evidence that "Ummah" refers to the community of human beings.

Therefore, in all instances where the word "Ummah" is used in the Holy Quran, this meaning of a population or group is intended, except in some exceptional cases accompanied by a specific context where "Ummah" is used metaphorically to mean a school of thought, such as: "Indeed, we found our fathers upon a religion (Ummah), and we are guided by their footsteps" (Az-Zukhruf: 22).

It is noteworthy that the content of this verse appears with a slight variation in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 92: "Indeed, this Ummah of yours is one Ummah, and I am your Lord, so worship Me." This follows the accounts of many Prophets, and in fact, "this" (hadhihi) refers to the nations of the previous Prophets, who were all one Ummah from the perspective of the Divine command, moving toward a single goal.

The next verse—following the call to unity and oneness in the previous verse—warns humans against fragmentation and discord with this expression: "But they tore their affair between them into sects" (فَتَقَطَّعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ زُبُرًا). And strangely, "each party rejoices in what it has with itself" and is averse to others (كُلُّ حِزْبٍ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ).

Zubur is the plural of zubrah (rhyming with luqmah), meaning a portion of hair on the back of an animal's head that is gathered and separated from the rest. Subsequently, this word was applied to anything that becomes segregated from another. Therefore, the phrase "they tore their affair between them into sects" refers to the fragmentation of nations into various groups.

Some have also suggested that Zubur might be the plural of Zabur, meaning "book," implying that each of them followed one of the heavenly books and rejected the rest of the Divine books, even though they all originated from a single source. However, the phrase "each party rejoices in what it has with itself" strengthens the first interpretation, as it speaks of different parties and the prejudice of each regarding its own claims.

In any case, the above verse reveals an important psychological and social reality: the ignorant prejudice of parties and groups, where each has chosen a path and creed for themselves and closed the windows of their minds to any other word. They do not allow a new ray of light to shine into their brains, a breeze to blow upon their souls, or a truth to be clarified for them!

This state, which stems from selfishness, excessive self-love, vanity, and self-conceit, is the greatest enemy to the clarification of truths and the achievement of harmony and unity among nations. This being "rejoiced" in one's own ways and feeling hatred and alienation toward everything else sometimes reaches a point where if a person hears a word contrary to his own way, he puts his fingers in his ears, wraps his garment over his head, and flees, lest a truth contrary to what he is accustomed to becomes clear to him. This is exactly what the Quran describes regarding the polytheists of the era of Nuh عليه السلام: "And indeed, every time I invited them that You may forgive them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted, and were arrogant with [great] arrogance" (Nuh: 7).

Until this state is ended, the path to reaching the Truth is impossible for man, and everyone will persist in their own way and remain obstinate.

Therefore, in the final verse under discussion, it says: "So leave them in their confusion for a time" (فَذَرْهُمْ فِي غَمْرَتِهِمْ حَتَّى حِينٍ). Now that this is the case, let them remain immersed in their ignorance, misguidance, negligence, and wandering until their death arrives or they are seized by Divine punishment; for such individuals have no fate other than this. The word hin (time) may refer to the time of death, the time of the descent of punishment, or both.

The word ghamrah (rhyming with darbah) originally comes from ghamr, meaning to erase the trace of something. Subsequently, a large amount of water that washes away its path as it moves forward is called ghamr or ghamir. Later, it was applied to ignorance and the troubles that submerge a person. In the verse under discussion, it means negligence, wandering, ignorance, and misguidance.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Volume 4, Chapters on Supplication, Chapter 67, Hadith 4. [^2]: We have discussed this in detail in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 186 of Surah Al-Baqarah. [^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Volume 7, page 4519 (under the verse in question).

Ayahs

أَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّمَا نُمِدُّهُم بِهِۦ مِن مَّالٍ وَبَنِينَ (٥٥)
Do they suppose that whatever aid We provide them in regard to wealth and children [is because] (55)
نُسَارِعُ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ بَل لَّا يَشْعُرُونَ (٥٦)
We are eager to bring them good? No, they are not aware! (56)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ هُم مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ رَبِّهِم مُّشْفِقُونَ (٥٧)
Indeed those who are apprehensive for the fear of their Lord, (57)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٨)
and believe in the signs of their Lord, (58)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِرَبِّهِمْ لَا يُشْرِكُونَ (٥٩)
and do not ascribe partners to their Lord; (59)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْتُونَ مَآ ءَاتَوا۟ وَّقُلُوبُهُمْ وَجِلَةٌ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ رَٰجِعُونَ (٦٠)
who give whatever they give while their hearts tremble with awe that they are going to return to their Lord (60)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَهُمْ لَهَا سَـٰبِقُونَ (٦١)
—it is they who are zealous in [performing] good works and take the lead in them. (61)

Commentary

Those Who Hasten Toward Good Deeds

Since the previous verses spoke of the stubborn, prejudiced, and egoistic factions who cling solely to their own beliefs, are content with them, and have closed the doors of investigation to their intellects, the verses under discussion point to another of their self-centered delusions, stating:

"Do they think that because We support them with wealth and children..." (أَ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّما نُمِدُّهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ مالٍ وَ بَنِينَ).

"...it is because We are hastening to open the doors of good things for them?" (نُسارِعُ لَهُمْ فِي الْخَيْراتِ). Do they imagine that possessing abundant wealth and many children is a proof of their clear righteousness and a sign of proximity and greatness in the presence of Allah?

No, it is never so; "rather, they do not perceive" (بَلْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ).

They do not realize that this abundance of wealth and children is, in reality, a form of punishment and retribution, or a prelude to punishment and penalty for them. They do not know that Allah intends to immerse them in luxury and blessings so that when they are seized by the grip of Divine punishment, enduring the torment will be more painful for them. For if the doors of blessings were closed to a person and they were prepared to accept hardships, the punishments would not be as painful. However, if someone is pulled out from the midst of luxury and comfort and cast into the dungeon of a terrifying prison, it will be exceptionally painful.

Furthermore, this abundance of blessings thickens the veils of negligence and pride over their eyes to the point where the path of return becomes impossible for them.

This is the very thing referred to in other verses of the Quran as "gradual leading to ruin through blessings" (istidraj) [1]. Additionally, the word "numiddu" (نمد) is derived from the root "imdad" and "madd," meaning to complete the deficiency of something and to prevent its cessation or end.

After negating the delusions of these negligent and egoistic individuals, the Quran describes the state of the believers and those who hasten toward good deeds through several verses, outlining their fundamental qualities: First, It says: "Indeed, those who are apprehensive from the fear of their Lord" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ هُمْ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ رَبِّهِمْ مُشْفِقُونَ).

It is noteworthy that "khashyah" (خشية) does not mean just any kind of fear; rather, it is a fear accompanied by veneration and respect. Similarly, "mushfiqun" (مشفقون), derived from "ishfaq" and the root "shafaq" (meaning light mixed with darkness), refers to a fear and apprehension that is blended with love and respect.

Since "khashyah" mostly pertains to the heart and "ishfaq" encompasses the practical aspect, the mention of these two as cause and effect in the verse becomes clear. In reality, It says: they are those in whose hearts the fear mixed with the greatness of Allah has taken root, and its effects are manifest in their actions and their vigilance regarding Divine commands. In other words, "ishfaq" is the stage of perfection of "khashyah," which manifests in action and compels one to avoid sin and fulfill responsibilities.

Then It adds: "And those who believe in the signs of their Lord" (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ).

After the stage of faith in the signs of the Lord comes the stage of sanctifying Him and considering Him free from any peer or partner, stating: "And those who do not associate partners with their Lord" (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ بِرَبِّهِمْ لا يُشْرِكُونَ). In fact, the negation of polytheism (shirk) is the result of faith in the signs of the Lord and its effect. Or, in other words, faith in the signs of the Lord refers to His "Affirmative Attributes" (Sifat al-Thubutiyyah), and the negation of shirk refers to His "Negative Attributes" (Sifat al-Salbiyyah). In any case, the negation of all forms of shirk—whether manifest or hidden—is included in this sentence.

Following this comes the stage of faith in the Hereafter and the Resurrection, and the special attention that true believers pay to this matter—an attention that completely regulates them in action. It says: "And those who give what they give [of charity and good deeds] while their hearts are fearful because they are to return to their Lord" (وَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْتُونَ ما آتَوْا وَ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَجِلَةٌ أَنَّهُمْ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ راجِعُونَ).

These are not like narrow-minded and low-aspiring individuals who, by performing a small deed, imagine themselves among those close to Allah's presence and develop such a state of vanity that they see everyone else as small and insignificant compared to themselves. Rather, even if they perform the greatest of righteous deeds—a deed equivalent to the worship of all humans and jinn—they say, like Ali عليه السلام:

"Ah, for the lack of provisions and the length of the journey!" (آه من قلة الزاد و بعد السفر).

After describing these four qualities, It says: "It is these who hasten to good deeds, and they are foremost in them" (أُولئِكَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْراتِ وَ هُمْ لَها سابِقُونَ). In reality, true good deeds, virtues, and real happiness are not what those drowned in luxury and the arrogant ones negligent of the world imagine. Goodness, happiness, and blessing belong to the group of believers who possess the aforementioned ideological and moral characteristics and, consequently, take the lead in performing righteous deeds.

The above verses provide a beautiful depiction and a perfectly logical arrangement to express the qualities of this group of pioneering believers. It begins with fear mixed with respect and veneration, which is the motivation for faith in the Lord and the negation of any shirk, and ends with faith in the Resurrection and the Court of Divine Justice, which results in a sense of responsibility and the motivation for every good deed. Altogether, it states four characteristics and one result (observe closely).

Furthermore, the expression "yusari'un" (يسارعون), which is from the form of "mufa'alah" and means speed for the sake of outstripping one another, is a beautiful expression that characterizes the state of the believers in a race toward a great and valuable destination. It shows how they engage in constructive competition and an unceasing race with one another in the program of righteous deeds.

[1]: For further explanation regarding "istidraj," please refer to Volume 7, page 32 (under verse 182 of Surah Al-A'raf).

Ayahs

وَلَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۖ وَلَدَيْنَا كِتَـٰبٌ يَنطِقُ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٦٢)
We task no soul except according to its capacity, and with Us is a book that speaks the truth, and they will not be wronged. (62)
بَلْ قُلُوبُهُمْ فِى غَمْرَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا وَلَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلٌ مِّن دُونِ ذَٰلِكَ هُمْ لَهَا عَـٰمِلُونَ (٦٣)
Indeed, their hearts are in a stupor in regard to this, and there are other deeds besides which they perpetrate. (63)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَخَذْنَا مُتْرَفِيهِم بِٱلْعَذَابِ إِذَا هُمْ يَجْـَٔرُونَ (٦٤)
When We seize their affluent ones with punishment, behold, they make entreaties [to Us]. (64)
لَا تَجْـَٔرُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ إِنَّكُم مِّنَّا لَا تُنصَرُونَ (٦٥)
‘Do not make entreaties today! Indeed, you will not receive any help from Us. (65)
قَدْ كَانَتْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَكُنتُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَـٰبِكُمْ تَنكِصُونَ (٦٦)
Certainly My signs used to be recited to you, but you used to take to your heels, (66)
مُسْتَكْبِرِينَ بِهِۦ سَـٰمِرًا تَهْجُرُونَ (٦٧)
being disdainful of it, talking nonsense in your nightly sessions.’ (67)

Commentary

Hearts Immersed in Ignorance!

Since the prominent and special qualities of the believers—which are the source of every good deed and were mentioned in the preceding verses—might raise the question that possessing such attributes and performing such deeds is not for everyone and is beyond the capacity of many, the first verse under discussion provides the answer, stating: "We do not task any soul except according to its capacity," and We expect from everyone only to the extent of their intellect and strength (وَلَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا).

This expression indicates that divine duties and rulings are within the limits of human ability, and in any case where they exceed the level of power and capacity, they are waived. In the terminology of the scholars of Usul (principles of jurisprudence), this rule governs all Islamic rulings and takes precedence over them.

Furthermore, since the question may arise as to how the deeds of all these humans, small and large, are accounted for and reviewed, it adds:

"And with Us is a Record which speaks with truth (and records and recounts all the deeds of the servants), and for that reason, no injustice or oppression shall be done to them" (وَلَدَيْنَا كِتَابٌ يَنْطِقُ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ).

This refers to the scrolls of deeds and the files in which all of man's actions are recorded and preserved with Allah; files that, as it were, have a tongue and recount the truth in such a way that no room remains for any denial. [^1] There is also the possibility that this "Record" which is with the Lord refers to the Lawh al-Mahfuz (the Preserved Tablet), and the expression "with Us" (لَدَيْنَا) supports this interpretation.

In any case, the above verse conveys the reality that not an atom's weight of man's deeds is consigned to oblivion, and everything will be precisely recorded. Faith in this reality encourages the doers of good in their righteous work and warns them against evil deeds.

The phrase "speaks with truth" (يَنْطِقُ بِالْحَقِّ) used to describe the record of man's deeds is similar to an expression we have in Persian when we say: "Such and such a letter is sufficiently eloquent," meaning it needs no further explanation or commentary; it is as if it speaks for itself and recounts the facts without the need for study.

The phrase "and they shall not be wronged" (وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ) also indicates that with the precise recording of deeds, there remains no place for injustice or oppression.

However, since the statement of these realities only affects those who possess a modicum of wakefulness and awareness, it immediately adds: "But the hearts of this stubborn disbelieving group are so immersed in ignorance and heedlessness that they are oblivious to the record of deeds, the Day of Reckoning and Reward, and what has come in the Quran of promises and warnings" (بَلْ قُلُوبُهُمْ فِي غَمْرَةٍ مِنْ هَذَا). [^2] This immersion and sinking into ignorance and heedlessness does not allow them to observe these clear realities, so that they might perhaps come to their senses and return to Allah. Then it adds: "And they have deeds besides that, which they are constantly doing" (وَلَهُمْ أَعْمَالٌ مِنْ دُونِ ذَلِكَ هُمْ لَهَا عَامِلُونَ).

Commentators have offered various interpretations for the phrase "they have deeds besides that" (لَهُمْ أَعْمَالٌ مِنْ دُونِ ذَلِكَ):

Some consider it a reference to the contrary and ugly deeds that stem from them due to ignorance and lack of knowledge (thus, "that" refers to their ignorance), and "deeds" refers to the sins they commit through this path.

Others have said: The meaning is that in addition to ideological disbelief, they also have much pollution in terms of action.

Finally, some have suggested the meaning is: The program of these disbelievers is completely separate from the program of the believers, and they follow two different lines.

Ultimately, these interpretations are not contradictory and can be reconciled. What must be noted is that the source of their shameful deeds is the placement of their hearts in a "state of immersion" (ghamrah) and sinking into ignorance and lack of knowledge.

But they remain in this state of heedlessness and ignorance "until the day We seize those among them who live in luxury (those drowned in comfort and blessings) with the grasp of punishment; at that time, their cry of entreaty will rise like the roar of wild beasts of the desert," and they will wail from the weight of the punishment and painful divine retribution (حَتَّى إِذَا أَخَذْنَا مُتْرَفِيهِمْ بِالْعَذَابِ إِذَا هُمْ يَجْأَرُونَ).

But they are addressed: "Do not cry out and do not wail today, for you shall not be helped by Us!" (لَا تَجْأَرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّكُمْ مِنَّا لَا تُنْصَرُونَ).

The specific mention of "those who live in luxury" (mutrafin) here—even though sinners are not limited to them—is either because they are the leaders of misguidance and error, or because the punishment will be more painful for them.

Additionally, the "punishment" intended here could be the punishment of this world, the punishment of the Hereafter, or both. That is, when the painful divine punishment in this world or the next encompasses them, they roar and cry out and entreat, but it is obvious that at that time, the matter is settled and there is no way back.

The next verse is, in fact, a statement of the cause of this grim fate; it says: "In the past, My verses were continuously recited to you, but instead of learning a lesson from them and waking up, you used to turn away and turn back on your heels" (قَدْ كَانَتْ آيَاتِي تُتْلَى عَلَيْكُمْ فَكُنْتُمْ عَلَى أَعْقَابِكُمْ تَنْكِصُونَ). "Tanksun" is from the root "nukus," meaning to retreat or go backward, and "a'qab" is the plural of "aqib," meaning the heel of the foot. This entire phrase is a metaphor for someone who hears an undesirable word and becomes so worried and terrified that he retreats backward on his heels.

Not only did you retreat upon hearing the divine verses, but "you assumed a state of arrogance against it" (مُسْتَكْبِرِينَ بِهِ). [^1]

[^1]: There is discussion among commentators as to what the pronoun "it" (bihi) refers to. Some have returned it to the Masjid al-Haram and the sanctuary of Mecca, considering that their arrogance was based on the fact that they saw themselves as the owners of the sanctuary and the custodians of the House of Allah. However, this possibility is weak because the preceding verses did not mention the Kaaba or the sanctuary at all. Rather, it is apparent that this pronoun refers to the Holy Quran or the Prophet; meaning you were arrogant toward the verses of the Quran and the Prophet of Islam, or it refers to their state of retreating—that by this retreat, you intended arrogance and indifference.

And in addition to this, "you held nighttime sessions and spoke ill of the Prophet, the Quran, and the believers" (سَامِرًا تَهْجُرُونَ).

"Samira" is from the root "samar," meaning nighttime conversations.

Some commentators have said the original meaning of this root is "the shadow of the moon at night," in which darkness and whiteness are mixed. Since nighttime conversations are sometimes held in the shadow of the moonlight—as is narrated about the Arab polytheists that they would gather around the Kaaba on moonlit nights and speak against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—this word has been used for it. And if we see that wheat-colored individuals or wheat itself is called "samra," it is because its whiteness is mixed with a bit of darkness.

"Tahjurun" is from the root "hajr," which originally means to distance oneself and separate. Later, it also came to mean the delirious speech of a sick person, because his words in that state are unpleasant and alienating. Also, "hujr" means profanity and insult, which is also a cause of distance and separation.

In the above verse, this latter meaning is intended; meaning you stay awake for a long time at night and, like sick people, speak deliriously and utter insults and profanities.

This is the way of illogical yet weak and base individuals; instead of relying on logic and evidence with courage in the light of day, they choose dark nights when people's eyes are asleep, and to advance their sinister goals or to soothe their inner discomforts and vent their frustrations, they resort to insults.

The Quran says: The cause of your misery and painful divine punishment was that you neither had the courage to accept the truth, nor did you humbly kneel before the verses of Allah, nor was your encounter with the Prophet a logical and correct encounter; for if it had been so, you would have found the path of truth.

Ayahs

أَفَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا۟ ٱلْقَوْلَ أَمْ جَآءَهُم مَّا لَمْ يَأْتِ ءَابَآءَهُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٦٨)
Have they not contemplated the Discourse, or has anything come to them [in it] that did not come to their forefathers? (68)
أَمْ لَمْ يَعْرِفُوا۟ رَسُولَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ (٦٩)
Is it that they do not recognize their apostle, and so they deny him? (69)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌۢ ۚ بَلْ جَآءَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كَـٰرِهُونَ (٧٠)
Do they say, ‘There is madness in him’? No, he has brought them the truth, and most of them are averse to the truth. (70)
وَلَوِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلْحَقُّ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ لَفَسَدَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهِنَّ ۚ بَلْ أَتَيْنَـٰهُم بِذِكْرِهِمْ فَهُمْ عَن ذِكْرِهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٧١)
Had the truth followed their desires, the heavens and the earth would have surely fallen apart [along] with those who are in them. Indeed, We have brought them their Reminder, but they are disregardful of their Reminder. (71)
أَمْ تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ خَرْجًا فَخَرَاجُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (٧٢)
Do you ask a recompense from them? Your Lord’s recompense is better, and He is the best of providers. (72)
وَإِنَّكَ لَتَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٧٣)
Indeed you invite them to a straight path, (73)
وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَنِ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ لَنَـٰكِبُونَ (٧٤)
and those who do not believe in the Hereafter surely deviate from the path. (74)

Commentary

The Various Excuses of the Deniers

Following the previous verses which spoke of the aversion and arrogance of the disbelievers toward the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), the verses under discussion address the excuses they might fabricate for themselves in this regard and provide crushing responses to them. Simultaneously, they explain the real causes of their aversion and turning away, which can be summarized into five parts:

First, He says: "Have they not reflected upon the Word (the divine verses)?" (أَ فَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا الْقَوْلَ‌). Indeed, the first factor of their wretchedness is the suspension of thought and reflection regarding the content of your invitation; had they reflected, their problems would have been resolved.

In the second stage, He says: "Or has there come to them that which did not come to their forefathers of old?" (أَمْ جاءَهُمْ ما لَمْ يَأْتِ آباءَهُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ‌). That is, if Monotheism (Tawhid), the Resurrection, and the call to goodness and purity had originated solely from you, they might have excused themselves by saying these are unprecedented words that we cannot accept.

If these matters were the truth, why did God—Who looks upon all human beings with grace and mercy—not send them to those who came before? However, considering that the content of your invitation, in terms of principles and foundations, is the same as the invitation of all the prophets, these excuses are meaningless.

In the third stage, He says: "Or is it that they did not recognize their Messenger, so they are deniers of him?" (أَمْ لَمْ يَعْرِفُوا رَسُولَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَهُ مُنْكِرُونَ‌). Meaning, if this call had been made by a mysterious or suspicious person, they might have said his words are true, but he himself is an unknown and mysterious figure, and one cannot be deceived by the appearance of his words.

But they know your history well; in the past, they called you "Muhammad the Trustworthy" (al-Amin) and confessed to your intellect, knowledge, and integrity. They know your parents and your tribe well; thus, there is no room for such excuses either.

In the fourth stage, He says: "Or do they say, 'In him is madness'?" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ). Meaning, we know his person well—he is not suspicious or mysterious—but we do not believe in his intellect and thought; perhaps he speaks these words out of madness, for they do not harmonize with the public opinion of the environment, and this lack of harmony and breaking of traditions is itself proof of madness!

The Quran immediately rejects this excuse by saying: "Rather, he has brought them the truth," and his words bear witness to this reality (بَلْ جاءَهُمْ بِالْحَقِ‌). "The fault lies here: that they are averse to the truth!" (وَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لِلْحَقِ‌ كارهُونَ‌).

Indeed, these are wise words; however, because they do not align with the whimsical desires of this group, they reject them and label them as insane thoughts!

While there is no necessity for the truth to follow the desires of the people, for "If the truth had followed their desires, and the universe revolved according to their whims, the heavens and the earth and whoever is in them would have been corrupted!" (وَ لَوِ اتَّبَعَ الْحَقُّ أَهْواءَهُمْ لَفَسَدَتِ السَّماواتُ وَ الْأَرْضُ وَ مَنْ فِيهِنَ‌). Because the whims and desires of people have no standard or criterion; rather, in many cases, they incline toward evil. If the laws of existence followed these deviant desires, chaos and corruption would encompass the entire world!

Then, to emphasize this point further, He says: "Rather, We have given them a Quran which is a source of reminder, awakening, and attention to God, and a source of honor, reputation, and status for them, but they turn away from that which is their reminder and honor" (بَلْ أَتَيْناهُمْ بِذِكْرِهِمْ فَهُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِهِمْ مُعْرِضُونَ‌) [1]

In the fifth and final stage, He says: "Is the excuse for their flight from the truth that you ask them for a reward, wage, or expense for your invitation? While the provision of your Lord is better for you, and He is the best of providers."

[1]: The expression "بِذِكْرِهِمْ" (their reminder/mention) may be interpreted as their awakening and being reminded, or it may mean their honor, status, and mention in human society. At the same time, these two meanings are not contradictory, and we have utilized both in the commentary of the verse.

(أَمْ تَسْأَلُهُمْ خَرْجاً فَخَراجُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَ هُوَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ‌) [1] Undoubtedly, if a spiritual and religious leader were to ask people for a material reward in exchange for his invitation, besides giving an excuse to the excuse-seekers to distance themselves due to lack of financial means, it would make them suspect that he has turned the call to truth into a shop for seeking material benefits.

Furthermore, what does man have to give to another? Is not all sustenance and provision in the hands of the Powerful Provider, Allah? In any case, the Quran, through the eloquent explanation provided in these five stages, makes it clear that these blind-hearted people are not submissive to the truth, and the excuses they mention to justify their opposition are nothing more than baseless pretexts.

In the next verse, as a general conclusion from what has passed, it says: "And most certainly, you invite them to a straight path" (وَ إِنَّكَ لَتَدْعُوهُمْ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌). A straight path whose signs are manifest, and with the slightest attention, its smoothness becomes clear.

We know that a straight path is the shortest distance between two points, and there is only one such path, while the deviant roads located to its left and right are infinite.

[1]: "Kharj" and "Kharaj" are derived from the root "khuruj" (to exit), meaning something that exits from a person's wealth or from agricultural land. However, "Kharj" has a broader meaning than "Kharaj." As Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Kharj" is the opposite of "Dakhl" (income), while "Kharaj" is usually the tax or rent determined for land.

Although in some Islamic narrations the "Straight Path" (Sirat al-Mustaqim) is interpreted as the Wilayah (guardianship) of Ali (عليه السلام) [1], as we have said many times, such narrations express some of the clear instances (misdaq) and do not contradict the existence of other instances such as the Quran, faith in the Origin and the Resurrection, piety, Jihad, and justice.

And the natural result of this matter is what the next verse recounts: "And those who do not believe in the Hereafter are surely deviating from this path" (وَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ عَنِ الصِّراطِ لَناكِبُونَ‌). "Nakib" is from the root "nakb" and "nukub," meaning deviation from the course, and "the misfortune (nakbat) of the world" as opposed to the world's favor means the world's deviation and turning its back.

It is clear that the "Path" (Sirat) intended in this verse is the same "Straight Path" mentioned in the previous verse. It is also certain that whoever deviates from the straight path of truth in this world will also deviate from the path to Paradise in the next world and fall into Hell, because everything there is the direct result of actions here. Emphasizing the lack of faith in the Hereafter and its connection to deviating from the path of truth is because a person does not feel responsibility until he has faith in the Resurrection.

In a tradition from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), we read: "Indeed, Allah has made us His gates, His path, His way, and the direction from which He is approached. So whoever deviates from our Wilayah or prefers others over us, they are deviating from the path." [1]

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 548.

Key Points

1. Worship of Truth vs. Worship of Desires

In the above verses, there was a brief and profound reference to the conflict between worshipping the truth and worshipping desires, stating: if the truth followed the whims and desires of the people, not only the earth and its inhabitants but also the heavens would be drawn into corruption. Analyzing this issue is not very difficult because:

  1. Undoubtedly, the whims and desires of people are not identical and often conflict with one another. It even happens frequently that the desires of a single individual are contradictory. If the truth were to submit to these inclinations, the result would be nothing but chaos, disintegration, and corruption. For each of them worships a deity and has fashioned an idol for themselves; if the truth were to submit to these demands and these scattered deities were to rule over the expanse of existence, its corruption would be hidden from no one.

  2. The whimsical desires of people are often directed toward matters that (regardless of their contradictions) are also harmful. If these inclinations were to give direction to the universe and human society, they would produce nothing but corruption.

  3. Whimsical desires are always one-dimensional and look only at one angle, neglecting other aspects. We know that one of the major factors of corruption is one-dimensional planning where other dimensions are never taken into consideration.

The above verse is not unlike what appears in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 22: "Had there been within them (the heavens and earth) gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined."

[1]: Usul al-Kafi (as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 549).

It is obvious that "Truth," like the "Straight Path," is one and unique. It is the whims and desires that, like imaginary gods, are numerous and many. Since this is the case, in the conflict and struggle between "Truth" and "Desire," which one should be followed? Desire, which is the cause of corruption for the heavens, the earth, and all creatures, or Truth, which is the secret of unity, Monotheism, order, and harmony? The result of this analysis and the answer to this question are perfectly clear.

2. Attributes of a Leader

From the above verses, some of the attributes of the leaders of the path of truth become clear: They were always people known for goodness. If they were unknown or mysterious individuals, then according to the verse "Or is it that they did not recognize their Messenger, so they are deniers of him," an excuse would fall into the hands of the hypocrites to ignore and deny their recognized invitation because of their own anonymity.

Another point is that they never submit to the whims of the people in their path. Contrary to what the modern world favors, they do not absolutely endorse public inclinations (even if they are deviant). They insist on promoting the school of truth even if it is unpleasant to a large group.

Furthermore, they do not seek material rewards for their invitation. They endure various deprivations and do not develop material dependence on anyone, for this need and dependence would become a chain on their hands and feet and a firm lock on their tongues and thoughts!

3. Why does the majority not incline toward the truth? Which majority?

In many verses of the Quran—such as the verses above—the "majority" is blamed and rebuked, while we know that in today's world, the majority of societies is considered the criterion for judging and measuring good and bad. This matter raises many questions.

Here, we are not speaking of verses that mention the pronoun "hum" (them) after the word "akthar" (most), which often refer to the disbelievers, polytheists, and their like, as that is outside our topic. Rather, we are speaking of verses that use the title "akthar al-nas" (most people), such as: "But most of the people are not grateful" (Al-Baqarah: 243). "But most of the people do not know" (Al-A'raf: 187). "But most of the people do not believe" (Hud: 17). "And most of the people, although you should desire it, are not believers" (Yusuf: 103). "But most of the people refuse [anything] except disbelief" (Al-Isra: 89). "And if you obey most of those upon the earth, they will mislead you from the way of Allah" (Al-An'am: 116).

On the other hand, in some verses of the Quran, the way and custom of the majority of believers is considered a correct criterion. In Surah An-Nisa, verse 115, we read: "And whoever opposes the Messenger after guidance has become clear to him and follows other than the way of the believers—We will give him what he has taken and drive him into Hell, and evil it is as a destination."

In Islamic narrations, regarding the discussion of conflicting reports, we see that one of the criteria for preference is "fame" (shuhrah) among the companions and followers of the Imams of Guidance. As Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) said: "Look at which of the two narrations from us regarding that which they judged is agreed upon by your companions; then take that which is our judgment and leave the rare (shadh) which is not famous among your companions, for that which is agreed upon has no doubt in it." [1] And in Nahj al-Balagha we read: "And adhere to the great mass (al-sawad al-a'zam), for Allah's hand is with the community (jama'ah). Beware of division, for the one isolated from the people is for Satan, just as the sheep isolated from the flock is for the wolf!" [2] Also in Nahj al-Balagha: "And adhere to that which the bond of the community is tied upon." [3]

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, vol. 18, p. 76 (Book of Judgment, Chapter 9 on the Attributes of a Judge). [2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 127. [3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 151.

In this way, some might imagine a contradiction between these two groups of verses and narrations. Furthermore, the thought might arise that Islam cannot reconcile with democratic government, because the foundation of democracy is based on the votes of the majority of people, which the Quran severely rebukes.

However, with a little reflection on those same verses and narrations cited above and comparing them with one another, their real meaning and concept become clear: The heart of the matter is that if the majority are believers, aware, and on the path of truth, their opinions are respected and often correspond to reality, and they must be followed.

But if the majority are unaware, ignorant, and uninformed, or aware but submissive to whims and desires, their opinions often have a deviant aspect, and following them—as the Quran says—leads man to error and misguidance.

On this account, to achieve a "healthy democracy," one must first strive for the masses of society to become aware and faithful, then set the opinions of the majority as the criterion for advancing social goals. Otherwise, democracy based on the opinions of a misguided majority sends society to Hell.

It is also necessary to mention that, in our belief, even the opinions of an aware, mature, and faithful majority are respected only if they are not contrary to the divine command and the Book and the Sunnah of the Prophet.

It is worth noting that part of the compulsions of today's societies in seeking refuge in the votes of the majority stems from the fact that they have no other criterion to rely upon. They have not taken the Heavenly Books and the programs of the prophets into account; the only thing left for them is the masses of people. Since they do not have the power to bring awareness to these masses—and furthermore, it is often more profitable for them that the masses remain unaware so they can easily lead them through propaganda—they have set the numerical majority as the criterion to silence the outcries.

If we reflect correctly on the state of today's societies and the laws and systems governing them, we will see that many of the miseries that have afflicted them are due to giving official status to the opinions of an unaware majority. What ugly and filthy laws—the mere mention of which is shameful—have been passed by the vote of the majority?! What fires have been ignited by the theory of an unaware majority?! And what injustices and tyrannies has a non-believing majority not endorsed?!

Ayahs

۞ وَلَوْ رَحِمْنَـٰهُمْ وَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِم مِّن ضُرٍّ لَّلَجُّوا۟ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (٧٥)
Should We have mercy upon them and remove their distress, they would surely persist, bewildered in their rebellion. (75)
وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْعَذَابِ فَمَا ٱسْتَكَانُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمْ وَمَا يَتَضَرَّعُونَ (٧٦)
We have already seized them with punishment, yet they did not humble themselves before their Lord, nor will they entreat [Him for mercy] (76)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَابًا ذَا عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ إِذَا هُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ (٧٧)
until We open on them the gate of a severe punishment, whereupon they will be despondent in it. (77)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٨)
It is He who made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts. Little do you thank. (78)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى ذَرَأَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٧٩)
It is He who created you on the earth, and you will be mustered toward Him. (79)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ وَلَهُ ٱخْتِلَـٰفُ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٨٠)
And it is He who gives life and brings death, and due to Him is the alternation of day and night. Do you not exercise your reason? (80)

Commentary

Various Methods of Divine Awakening

Since the previous verses discussed the various excuses the deniers of truth put forward to evade the call of the prophets, in the verses under discussion, Allah speaks of the ways of establishing the proof (atmam al-hujjah) and awakening them.

First, He says: Sometimes We encompass them with Our blessings so they may wake up, but "even if We were to show them mercy and remove whatever affliction they have, they would persist blindly in their transgression" (وَلَوْ رَحِمْنَاهُمْ وَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِم مِّن ضُرٍّ لَّلَجُّوا فِي طُغْيَانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ).

And sometimes We discipline them with painful events so that if they were not awakened through mercy and blessing, they might be awakened through this path; however, even this is not effective for them, because "We seized them with punishment, yet they did not humble themselves before their Lord, nor do they entreat [Him]" (وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَاهُم بِالْعَذَابِ فَمَا اسْتَكَانُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ وَمَا يَتَضَرَّعُونَ) [1]. As we have mentioned before, the word tadarru’ (تضرع) is originally derived from the root dar’ (ضرع), meaning "teat" or "udder," and tadarru’ refers to the act of milking; subsequently, it came to mean submission mixed with humility and lowliness.

This means that these painful events never brought them down from the steed of pride, rebellion, and self-will, and they did not submit to the Truth.

And if in some narrations tadarru’ is interpreted as raising the hands during supplication and prayer, it is actually an expression of one of the instances of this broad meaning.

In any case, We continue these mercies, blessings, and awakening punishments, and they continue their transgression, rebellion, and stubbornness, "until We open for them a gate of severe punishment, and then they will be plunged into utter despair" (حَتَّىٰ إِذَا فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَابًا ذَا عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ إِذَا هُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ) [1].

In reality, Allah has two types of punishment: "educational punishments" and "punishments of cleansing and eradication (istisal)." The goal of the first type is to place them in hardship and suffering so they may realize their weakness and inability and dismount from the steed of pride.

However, the goal of the second type, which occurs regarding incorrigible individuals, is to uproot them by the decree of the command of creation; for in this system, the right to life no longer remains for them, and these thorns on the path of human perfection must be cleared away.

Regarding what is meant by "a gate of severe punishment" (بَابًا ذَا عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ), there is discussion among commentators:

Many have considered it to be death, followed by the punishment and retribution of the Resurrection.

Others have considered it an allusion to the severe famine that, due to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) curse, afflicted the polytheists for several years, to the point that food became entirely unavailable and they were forced to eat things that no one would normally be willing to eat.

Others consider it an allusion to the painful retribution that fell upon them under the sword-strokes of the warriors of Islam on the field of "Badr."

It is also possible that this verse does not refer to a specific group but rather recounts a general and universal law regarding Divine punishments, which begins with mercy, followed by disciplinary warnings and punishments, and finally the punishment of eradication and destructive retribution [2].

[^1]: "Istakanu" (استكانوا) is from the root sukun (سكون), meaning to become still in a state of humility and submissiveness. In this case, it would be from the form of Ifti'al, which was originally istakanu, and the fatha of the kaf was elongated and turned into an alif, resulting in istakanu. Some have said it is from the root kawn (كون) from the form of Istif'al, meaning seeking stability in a place accompanied by humility and submissiveness. In any case, it reflects the state of the servant's humility before the Lord. The fact that some have mentioned it as meaning "supplication" is because supplicating is one of the instances of humility and modesty. There is also a third possibility that it is from the root kayn (كين) from the form of Istif'al, as this root has been used to mean humility. All these meanings are close in scope.

[^1]: "Mublis" (مبلس) is from the root iblas (ابلاس), meaning a sorrow that occurs due to the severity of an event and often leads a person to silence and bewilderment or hopelessness and despair.

[^2]: The verse "Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter..." which precedes these verses, supports this interpretation.

After this explanation, the Quran enters from another path and proceeds to mention Divine blessings to stimulate their sense of gratitude, saying: "It is He who created for you hearing, sight, and hearts (intellect); little is the thanks you give" (وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنشَأَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصَارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ). The emphasis on these three subjects (hearing, sight, and intellect) is because these three are the primary tools of human cognition. One perceives sensory matters mostly through sight and hearing, and non-sensory matters through the power of the intellect.

To realize the importance of these two external senses (sight and hearing), it is enough to consider the state that befalls a person due to the loss of these two; to what extent his world becomes limited and void of any light, brightness, awakening, and awareness. Even due to the loss of these two, he practically loses many of his other senses. The tongue and speech, which initially function through hearing and serve as the link between man and others, can no longer perform their task (those born deaf are always mute, even though their tongues have no defect).

Thus, these two senses are the keys to the world of the sensible. Then comes the turn of the intellect, which is the key to the world beyond the senses and the metaphysical world, and at the same time, it is tasked with critiquing, concluding, summarizing, generalizing, and analyzing the outputs of those two senses.

Are those who do not give thanks for these three great means of cognition not worthy of reproach and blame? And is not the contemplation of the intricacies of these three effective means sufficient to acquaint man with their Creator?

If the blessings of hearing and sight are mentioned before the intellect in the above verse, the reason is clear. But why does He prioritize the blessing of hearing over sight? It may be for the reason that, according to scientists, the infant's hearing functions for the first time, and sight some time after that; for closed eyes in the dark environment of the womb have no readiness to observe light waves, and for this reason, they remain closed for a long time after birth to gradually get used to the light, whereas the ear is not like this; even according to some, it has the power of hearing in the fetal world [1] and hears the sound of the mother's heart.

In truth, the mention of the three aforementioned favors is a motivation for recognizing the Giver of these favors, which sends one in pursuit of knowing the original Benefactor (just as scholars of theology have considered the discussion of "gratitude to the Benefactor" as a basis for the intellectual necessity of knowing Allah). Then, in the next verse, it proceeds to one of the most important signs of the Lord, namely the creation of man from this earthy ground, saying: "It is He who scattered you across the earth" (وَهُوَ الَّذِي ذَرَأَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ) [2], and since you were created from the earth, you shall return to the earth, and once again "unto Him you shall be gathered" (وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ).

And if you reflect upon your own creation from worthless dust, it is sufficient for you to know the Giver of existence and also to realize the possibility of the issue of the Resurrection.

[^1]: We have spoken about the three tools of cognition in Volume 11, page 335 onwards (under verse 78 of Surah An-Nahl).

[^2]: "Dhara'akum" (ذرأكم) is from the root dhara' (ذرء), which originally means creation, bringing into existence, and manifestation. However, the root dharw (ذرو) with a waw means scattering. These two roots should not be confused with each other, and the verse under discussion is from the first root (unfortunately, in Volume 7 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 19, an error occurred in this regard which we hereby correct).

After mentioning the issue of human creation, it proceeds to the issue of death and life and the alternation of night and day, which are among the great signs of the Lord, saying: "It is He who gives life and causes death, and to Him belongs the alternation of the night and the day. Will you not then understand?" (وَهُوَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ وَلَهُ اخْتِلافُ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهَارِ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ). Thus, in the last three verses, it begins with the motivation for knowing the Lord and ends the discussion by mentioning some of the most important internal (anfusi) and external (afaqi) signs; in other words, it recounts the journey of man from the beginning of birth until death and the return to the Lord, all of which takes place by His command and by His will.

Interestingly, He places the creation of "death" and "life" alongside the creation of "night" and "day," because light and darkness in the expanse of the universe are like death and life in the world of living beings. Just as in the light of waves of light, the universe finds bustle and movement, and under the veils of darkness it tends toward silence, so too living beings begin their movement with the light of life and are silenced with the darkness of death, and both aspects are gradual.

We have previously mentioned that the "alternation of night and day" may mean their coming and going, where each becomes the "successor" and replacement of the other, and it may also mean their gradual difference and variation which creates the four seasons of the year and leads the cycle of life in the world of plants under a precise system.

In any case, all these matters can be a guide on the path of knowing the Lord.

For this reason, at the end of the verse, He says: "Will you not then understand?" (أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ).

Commentary

The Quran Summons Their Conscience to Judgment

The preceding verses invited the deniers of Divine Unity and the Resurrection to contemplate the universe and the signs within the horizons and within themselves. The verses under discussion add: they have abandoned thought and intellect, blindly imitating their ancestors; "Rather, they say like what the former people said" (بَلْ قالُوا مِثْلَ ما قالَ الْأَوَّلُونَ).

"They said out of wonder: 'When we have died and become dust and bones, will we indeed be resurrected?'" (قالُوا أَ إِذا مِتْنا وَ كُنَّا تُراباً وَ عِظاماً أَ إِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ) [1]

This is unbelievable! "We have been promised this, we and our forefathers, before" (لَقَدْ وُعِدْنا نَحْنُ وَ آباؤُنا هذا مِنْ قَبْلُ). "This is nothing but the legends and myths of the ancients" (إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

Re-creation is a myth, the Reckoning is another legend, and Paradise and Hell are each nothing more than a fable!

Since the disbelievers and polytheists feared the issue of the Resurrection more than anything else—and for this reason, they sought to evade it through various excuses and clever ruses—the Quran speaks of the Resurrection in detail and with emphasis. Therefore, in the continuation of the verses under discussion, it shatters the hollow logic of the deniers of the Resurrection through three paths: through Allah's ownership over the expanse of the universe, then His Lordship (Rububiyyah), and finally His sovereignty over the entirety of the world. From all these discussions, it concludes that He possesses the power and ability for the Resurrection from every aspect, and His justice and wisdom necessitate that this world be followed by the Hereafter.

Interestingly, in each case, it extracts an admission from the polytheists themselves and turns their own words back upon them.

First, it says: "Say, 'To whom belongs the earth and whoever is in it, if you should know?'" (قُلْ لِمَنِ الْأَرْضُ وَ مَنْ فِيها إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ).

Then it adds that based on the call of their primordial nature (fitrah) and their belief in Allah as the Creator of existence, "They will say, '[It belongs] to Allah'" (سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ). But you "Say, 'Then will you not remember?'" (قُلْ أَ فَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ).

With this explicit and clear admission, how can you consider the revival of man after death to be far-fetched? And how can you deem it beyond the encompassing power of the Great Allah?

Once again, He commands to ask them: "Say, 'Who is Lord of the seven heavens and Lord of the Great Throne?'" (قُلْ مَنْ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ السَّبْعِ وَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ).

Again, based on that same monotheistic nature and their belief in Allah as the Creator of existence, "They will say, '[They belong] to Allah'" (سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ). With this manifest confession, "Say, 'Then will you not fear Him [and stop denying the Resurrection and man's return to life]?'" (قُلْ أَ فَلا تَتَّقُونَ).

Once more, ask them about the sovereignty over the heavens and the earth and "Say, 'In whose hand is the dominion of all things?'" (قُلْ مَنْ بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

"Who gives protection to all the defenseless and needs no protection from anyone?" (وَ هُوَ يُجِيرُ وَ لا يُجارُ عَلَيْهِ).

"If indeed you know [these realities]" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ).

Once again, they open their tongues in admission and "They will say, '[It belongs] to Allah [dominion, sovereignty, support, and protection in this world are exclusive to Him]'" (سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ). "Say, 'Then how are you deluded [into saying the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has bewitched you or that you are under a spell]?'" (قُلْ فَأَنَّى تُسْحَرُونَ).

These are realities that you yourselves admit at every stage: you consider Him the "Owner" of existence, you identify Him as the "Creator" of existence, and you regard Him as the "Manager, Director, Sovereign, and Refuge."

Can One who possesses such power and ability, and whose realm of sovereignty is so vast, not clothe a human being—who was initially dust and has returned to dust—with the garment of life again, and resurrect and gather him?
