Why do you evade the realities? Why do you consider the Prophet of Islam a sorcerer or a madman, when in the depths of your hearts you admit to these truths?

Finally, as a concise summary and conclusion, He says: It is neither sorcery, nor magic, nor anything else; "Rather, We have brought them the truth, and indeed, they are liars" (بَلْ أَتَيْناهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ).

There has been no shortcoming in the expression of truths from Our side or from Our prophets; the fault lies with you, who have closed your eyes, taken the path of deviation, and stubbornly and persistently continue upon it.

Key Points

1- Meaning of Several Words: [Asatir, Rabb, Malakut, 'Arsh]

"Asatir" (اساطير) is the plural of "Usturah" (اسطوره). According to linguists, it comes from the root "Satr" (سطر), which originally means "row" or "line." Therefore, words that are placed in a row—so to speak, lined up—are called a "Satr" (line).

Accordingly, "Usturah" means writings and lines left as a legacy by others. Since ancient writings often contain legends and superstitions, this word is usually applied to superstitious and false tales and stories. The word "Asatir" is repeated nine times in the Quran, all from the tongues of faithless disbelievers addressing the prophets to justify their opposition to them.

"Rabb" (رب), as we mentioned in the first volume in the commentary of Surah Al-Hamd, means "the Reforming Owner." Thus, it is not said of everyone who owns something, but rather of an owner who seeks to reform, preserve, and manage his property. For this reason, it has sometimes been used to mean "Nurturer" or "Educator."

"Malakut" (ملكوت) comes from the root "Mulk" (sovereignty and ownership), and the addition of "waw" and "ta" is for emphasis and hyperbole.

"Arsh" (عرش) means a high-based throne and is sometimes applied to a ceiling or trellis. When this word is used regarding the Lord, it means the "entirety of the universe," which is considered the throne of His government and rule. However, sometimes this word is applied only to the metaphysical world, in contrast to "Kursi" (the Chair), which refers to the world of nature and matter, as in: "His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth" (Al-Baqarah: 255). [1]

2- Resurrection Through the Universality of Allah's Power

It is clearly evident from the verses of the Quran that the greatest opposition of the deniers of the Resurrection was regarding the issue of the physical Resurrection and wonder at the return of a human who has become dust back to life. Therefore, many verses on the Resurrection emphasize the issue of Allah's power and explain examples of it in the universe so that their wonder regarding the issue of life after death may vanish.

In the verses under discussion, this same issue is pursued through three paths: first, His power regarding the earth and its inhabitants; then, the heavens and the Great Throne; and finally, His power over the management and administration of the world of creation. Thus, all three are instances of a single concept.

It is also possible that each of these three discussions refers to one of the viewpoints of the deniers of the Resurrection. It says: If your denial is because decayed and dusted humans fall outside the realm of Allah's ownership, this is a mistake, for you yourselves consider Allah the Owner of the earth and its inhabitants.

And if it is because you say the revival of the dead requires a Powerful Lord, you yourselves call Allah the Lord of the heavens and the Throne.

And if this denial is because you have objections regarding the management of the world after the revival of the dead, that too is misplaced, for you have accepted His power in managing this universe and providing refuge to all beings. Thus, no room remains for your denial.

The consistency of the disbelievers' response in the three cases (سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ) strengthens the first interpretation.

3- Difference in the Endings of the Verses

It is interesting that after mentioning the first question and answer, the phrase "Then will you not remember?" (أَ فَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ) appears. After the second question and answer, "Then will you not fear [Allah]?" (أَ فَلا تَتَّقُونَ) appears. And after the third question and answer, "Then how are you deluded?" (فَأَنَّى تُسْحَرُونَ) is mentioned.

In reality, these are rebukes and reproaches that become more intense stage by stage. This is one of the common methods in logical education and training: when one wishes to condemn someone with three proofs, they first reproach them mildly, then severely, and finally even more severely!

[^1]: We have discussed the "Arsh" in detail in Volume 6, page 204 (under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf).

Ayahs

مَا ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِن وَلَدٍ وَمَا كَانَ مَعَهُۥ مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ ۚ إِذًا لَّذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلَـٰهٍۭ بِمَا خَلَقَ وَلَعَلَا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (٩١)
Allah has not taken any offspring, neither is there any god besides Him, for then each god would take away what he created, and some of them would surely rise up against others. Clear is Allah of what they allege! (91)
عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٩٢)
The Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, He is above having any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (92)

Commentary

Polytheism Leads the World to Ruin

In the preceding verses, discussions regarding the Resurrection, as well as the ownership, sovereignty, and Lordship of the Almighty were presented. The verses under discussion address the issue of negating polytheism (shirk), highlighting some of the deviations of the polytheists and responding to them.

First, it states: "Allah has never taken a son, nor is there any other deity with Him" (مَا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ مِنْ وَلَدٍ وَ ما كانَ مَعَهُ مِنْ إِلهٍ‌).

The belief in God having a child is not exclusive to Christians who call Jesus His literal son! This belief also existed among the polytheists who imagined the angels to be the daughters of Allah. Perhaps the Christians adopted this idea from earlier polytheists. In any case, since a child is essentially and inherently a part of the father, they attributed a share of divinity to the angels or Prophet Jesus and others, which is one of the clearest manifestations of polytheism.

Then, it proceeds to provide the reasoning for the negation of polytheism, stating: "If Allah had a partner and multiple deities ruled the universe, each of these gods would have taken what he created under his own management and administration" (and naturally, every part of the world would be governed by a specific system, which is inconsistent with the unity of the system we observe governing it) (إِذاً لَذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلهٍ بِما خَلَقَ‌).

"Furthermore, each of these gods would have attempted to gain superiority over the others to expand his realm of sovereignty," and this itself would be another cause for the disintegration of the world's order (وَ لَعَلا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌).

At the end of the verse, as a general conclusion, it says: "Exalted is Allah above what they describe!" (سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ‌).

The essence of this argument is that we clearly observe a single system governing the expanse of existence. The laws governing this world are identical in the earth and the heavens. The laws that govern an exceedingly small atom also govern the solar system and other massive galaxies. As scientists say, if we were to enlarge an atom, it would take the shape of the solar system, and conversely, if we were to shrink the solar system, it would appear as an atom.

Studies conducted by experts in various sciences using modern instruments regarding distant worlds also indicate the unity of the overall system of the universe; this is one aspect.

On the other hand, the requirement of "plurality" is always a type of "divergence," because if two things were identical in every respect, they would become one thing, and duality would have no meaning.

Therefore, if we assume multiple gods for this world, this plurality would affect the creatures of the world and the system governing them, and the result would be the lack of unity in the system of creation.

Beyond this, every being seeks its own perfection, except for that Being who is perfect in every respect, for whom perfection has no meaning. In our discussion, where we assumed separate realms for the gods, naturally none of them possesses absolute perfection. It is natural that each would seek his own perfection and want to bring the entire universe under his own domain, and the consequence of this is the seeking of superiority by each over the other, resulting in the collapse of the system of existence.

In this way, each of the two phrases in the above verse refers to a logical proof, and it does not reach the point where we consider these arguments merely persuasive rather than logical. [1] The only question that remains here is: all of this applies if we assume the gods seek superiority, but if they are wise and aware, what prevents them from, for example, managing the world through a consultative system?!

[1]: The late Allamah Tabataba'i in Tafsir al-Mizan provides another interpretation for the phrase "وَ لَعَلا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌," the summary of which is: The systems governing the world are sometimes horizontal to one another, like the system governing the desert and the sea, and sometimes vertical, like the general system governing the entire solar system and the system governing the planet Earth, which is a part of that whole. In the second case, one of these systems is placed above the other. If each of them were dependent on a god, we must accept that the god governing the general system is superior in every case to the god governing the partial system. Thus, we would have to accept a hierarchy for the gods (like the hierarchy among ministers, governors, and mayors of a country!), and accepting a hierarchy for gods is impossible (Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 15, p. 66).

We have provided a detailed answer to this question in Volume 13 under verse 22 of Surah Al-Anbiya in the discussion of the "Proof of Mutual Hindrance" (Burhan al-Tamanu'), and we see no need for repetition.

The next verse is another response to these nonsensical-speaking polytheists: "It says: Allah is the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed (the hidden and the manifest)" (عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ). He is never aware of anything by the name of the gods you claim.

Is it possible for there to be another god in the world and you be aware of it, while Allah, who is your Creator and knows the unseen and the witnessed of the world, be unaware of it?

This statement is actually similar to the statement in verse 18 of Surah Yunus: قُلْ أَ تُنَبِّئُونَ اللَّهَ بِما لا يَعْلَمُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ لا فِي الْأَرْضِ‌: "Say, 'Do you inform Allah of something He does not know in the heavens or on the earth?'" Finally, with this phrase, He strikes a line of nullity through their superstitious imaginings: "Allah is exalted above what they say and the partners they associate with Him" (فَتَعالى‌ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ‌).

The end of this verse is exactly like the end of verse 18 of Surah Yunus; there we also read: سُبْحانَهُ وَ تَعالى‌ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ‌, and this shows that both verses pursue the same point.

This phrase is also a threat to the polytheists, indicating that Allah is aware of their inner and outer secrets, knows all these words, and will try and punish them in His court of justice in due time.

Ayahs

قُل رَّبِّ إِمَّا تُرِيَنِّى مَا يُوعَدُونَ (٩٣)
Say, ‘My Lord! If You should show me what they are promised, (93)
رَبِّ فَلَا تَجْعَلْنِى فِى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٩٤)
then do not put me, my Lord, among the wrongdoing lot.’ (94)
وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّرِيَكَ مَا نَعِدُهُمْ لَقَـٰدِرُونَ (٩٥)
We are indeed able to show you what We promise them. (95)
ٱدْفَعْ بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ ۚ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَصِفُونَ (٩٦)
Repel ill [conduct] with that which is the best. We know best whatever they allege. (96)
وَقُل رَّبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ هَمَزَٰتِ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ (٩٧)
Say, ‘My Lord! I seek Your protection from the promptings of devils, (97)
وَأَعُوذُ بِكَ رَبِّ أَن يَحْضُرُونِ (٩٨)
and I seek Your protection, my Lord, from their presence near me.’ (98)

Commentary

Seek Refuge in Allah from the Whispers of the Devils

Although the address in these verses is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), they pursue the objective of the preceding verses, which was to threaten the stubborn disbelievers and polytheists with divine punishments.

First, it says: "Say [O Prophet!]: 'My Lord! If You should show me that [punishment] which they are promised...'" (قُلْ رَبِّ إِمَّا تُرِيَنِّي ما يُوعَدُونَ) [^1]

"My Lord! Then do not place me among the wrongdoing people" (رَبِّ فَلا تَجْعَلْنِي فِي الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ). My request is that whenever Your definitive punishment overtakes them, grant me Your favor and deliver me from this place of destruction, so that I may not be associated with the oppressors and wrongdoers.

Undoubtedly, the actions and conduct of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) were not such that would subject him to divine punishment. Furthermore, it is certain that under the law of divine justice, the "wet and the dry" never burn together; even if in a vast land there were only one duty-bound God-worshipper and others were overtaken by punishment for their crimes, Allah would save that one person.

However, this prayer of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), performed by divine command, is for two reasons: firstly, to warn the polytheistic disbelievers that the matter is so serious that even the great Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) must entrust himself to Allah and seek salvation from Him; and secondly, it is a teaching for all followers of this Prophet never to feel secure from divine punishment and to entrust themselves to Him in every state.

As for what is meant by this punishment, most commentators believe it refers to the worldly punishments that Allah inflicted upon the polytheists, including the crushing defeat

[^1]: The word "Imma" (إِمَّا) in the above verse is composed of "In" (the conditional particle) and the "extra Ma" (Ma al-Za'idah), which is brought here for emphasis. Usually, for the conditional "In" to precede a verb accompanied by the "Noon of emphasis," the word "Ma" must act as a separator.

and the heavy blows dealt to them in the Battle of Badr [^1]. Considering that Surah al-Mu'minun is Meccan and that the believers were under severe pressure in those days, these verses served as a form of comfort and consolation for them (a similar meaning also appears in Surah Yunus, verse 46).

However, some commentators have suggested the possibility that it includes both the punishment of this world and the Hereafter [^2], but the first interpretation seems closer to the context.

To further emphasize this subject and negate any doubt or hesitation from the enemies, and to provide comfort and consolation to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers, the next verse adds: "And indeed, We are surely able to show you what We have promised them" (وَ إِنَّا عَلى‌ أَنْ نُرِيَكَ ما نَعِدُهُمْ لَقادِرُونَ). As we know, this power of the Lord was manifested in various scenes after that time—including the scene of the Battle of Badr—and an army that appeared very small and weak in power triumphed over a multitude of enemies by Allah's command and the strength of faith.

Then, He commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to deal with this group with forbearance and "Repel evil with that which is better [through forgiveness and kindness]; We are most knowing of what they describe" (ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ السَّيِّئَةَ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَصِفُونَ). Do not be in a hurry or haste in this path, and know that "We are most knowing of what they

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, al-Mizan, Fi Zilal, Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, and Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the discussed verses.

[^2]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, under the discussed verses.

say and describe."

We know that their unseemly actions, harsh words, and various forms of harassment distress you, but you are not duty-bound to retaliate in kind against those brutalities and foul words. Respond to evil with good, for this is one of the most effective methods for awakening the negligent and the deceived.

Yet, at the same time, entrust yourself to Allah "And say: 'My Lord! I seek refuge in You from the incitements (whispers) of the devils'" (وَ قُلْ رَبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ هَمَزاتِ الشَّياطِينِ).

Not only from their misleading whispers, but "And I seek refuge in You, my Lord, lest they be present with me" (وَ أَعُوذُ بِكَ رَبِّ أَنْ يَحْضُرُونِ). Lest they attend my gatherings, for their very presence is seductive and harmful.

Key Points

1- What are the "Hamzat" of the Devils?

"Hamzat" is the plural of "Hamzah," meaning to push or incite with force. The letter Hamzah is so named because it is expelled with force from the back of the throat. According to some commentators, "Hamz," "Ghamz," and "Ramz" all convey a similar meaning, except that "Ramz" refers to a slight stage, "Ghamz" is more intense, and "Hamz" expresses the ultimate intensity [^1]. Given that "Devils" (Shayatin) is plural, it includes all devils, whether manifest or hidden,

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi.

from among humans and Jinn.

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we read that the Imam (عليه السلام), regarding the meaning of the verse "Say: My Lord! I seek refuge in You from the incitements of the devils," said:

"It refers to the satanic whispering that falls into your heart." [^1] When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), despite possessing the station of infallibility (Ismah) and divine protection, makes such a request from Allah, the duty of others is clear. All believers must ask the Lord, who is their Master and Disposer of affairs, not to leave them to themselves for even a moment, so that they not only avoid being influenced by the whispers of the devils but also avoid their presence in their gatherings.

In this way, all wayfarers on the path of truth must constantly be wary of satanic suggestions and always place themselves under His protection in this regard.

2- Responding to Evil with Good!

One of the most effective ways to combat stubborn and persistent enemies is to respond to their evils with goodness. This is where a clamor arises from within their conscience, placing the wrongdoer under the heavy blows of self-reproach and blame, and in comparing himself with the opponent, he grants the right to the other side. This very matter, in many cases, causes the enemy to reconsider his plans.

In the biography of the leaders and the practical method of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Imams of Guidance (عليهم السلام), we have often seen that they responded with goodness and treated with affection individuals or groups who had committed the worst crimes, and this became the cause of a spiritual revolution and their return

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 552.

to the path of truth.

The Quran repeatedly—including in the above verses—reminds Muslims of this as a principle in fighting evil, and even in Surah Fussilat, verse 34, it says:

"The result of this action will be that the one between whom and you was enmity will become as though he was a devoted friend" (فَإِذَا الَّذِي بَيْنَكَ وَ بَيْنَهُ عَداوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُ وَلِيٌّ حَمِيمٌ).

However, it goes without saying that this instruction is specific to cases where the enemy does not abuse it, does not consider it a sign of weakness, and does not become more emboldened and audacious.

Furthermore, the concept of this statement is never compromise or accepting surrender to the whispers of the enemies; perhaps for this reason, immediately after stating this instruction in the above verses, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is commanded to seek refuge in Allah from the incitements and whispers of the devils and their presence.

Ayahs

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَحَدَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ قَالَ رَبِّ ٱرْجِعُونِ (٩٩)
When death comes to one of them, he says, ‘My Lord! Take me back, (99)
لَعَلِّىٓ أَعْمَلُ صَـٰلِحًا فِيمَا تَرَكْتُ ۚ كَلَّآ ۚ إِنَّهَا كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قَآئِلُهَا ۖ وَمِن وَرَآئِهِم بَرْزَخٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (١٠٠)
that I may act righteously in what I have left behind.’ ‘By no means! These are mere words that he says.’ And before them is a barrier until the day they will be resurrected. (100)

Commentary

An Impossible Request!

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding the stubbornness of the polytheists and sinners in their path of falsehood, the verses under discussion describe their painful state when they stand on the threshold of death:

"They continue in this wrong path of theirs until, when death comes to one of them" (حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ‌) [1]

[1] "حتی" (Until) is actually the conclusion for an omitted sentence understood from the previous context, and the implied meaning is: "They persist in this state until death comes to one of them." This meaning is derived from the phrase "نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَصِفُونَ‌" (We know best what they attribute) mentioned two verses prior (reflect upon this).

At this moment, when they see themselves being severed from this world and placed into the next, the veils of pride and negligence are lifted from before their eyes. It is as if they see their painful destiny with their own sight; they observe their lost life and resources, the shortcomings they committed in the past, the sins they perpetrated, and their ominous consequences. It is here that his cry rises and "he says: 'My Lord, send me back!'" (قالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ‌).

Send me back "so that I may do righteous deeds in that which I left behind" (لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ‌). But since the law of creation does not grant such permission to return to anyone—neither the righteous nor the wicked—he is answered: "No! There is never a way back" (كَلَّا).

"It is but a word he speaks" (إِنَّها كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قائِلُها).

A word that has never arisen from the depths of his heart with will and freedom; it is the same word that every criminal says when caught in the clutches of punishment, and every murderer says upon seeing the gallows. Whenever the waves of calamity subside, he continues his former program.

This is similar to what we read in Surah Al-An'am, verse 28: "وَ لَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ‌" (And if they were returned, they would certainly revert to that which they were forbidden).

At the end of the verse, a very brief and meaningful reference is made to the mysterious world of Barzakh (the Purgatory), saying: "And behind them is a barrier until the Day they are resurrected" (وَ مِنْ وَرائِهِمْ بَرْزَخٌ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- Who is the addressee in the phrase "رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ‌"?

Given that the word "رَبِّ" (Rabbi) is a contraction of "رَبّی" meaning "My Lord," the beginning of this sentence indicates that the addressee is Allah Almighty. However, since "ارْجِعُونِ" (send me back) is in the plural form, the addressee cannot be God [in a literal singular sense], and the combination of these two expressions in the above sentence has raised questions.

A group of commentators believes that the addressee is Allah, and the use of the plural form here is for respect and glorification, which is also common in our Persian language where we address a single person as "you" (plural) out of respect. However, considering that this expression was not common in the Arabic language, especially in ancient times, and no example of it is found in the Quran, the weakness of this interpretation is clear. [1] Another group of commentators has said that the addressees are actually the angels of death and the takers of souls, and saying the word "Lord" is a type of supplication to the threshold of God. This is frequent in our daily speech; when a person is in a severe crisis, they first supplicate to God and then seek help from people, crying out: "O God! O God! Save me, rush to my aid!"

This interpretation seems more correct.

[1] Some commentators have considered verse 9 of Surah Al-Qasas: "قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِي وَ لَكَ لا تَقْتُلُوهُ‌" (A comfort of the eye for me and for you; do not kill him), spoken by Pharaoh's wife when taking the cradle of Moses from the water, as an example of this expression. At its beginning, the addressee is Pharaoh, and in the continuation, the addressees are his entourage who were commissioned to kill the sons of Israel (reflect upon this).

2- Commentary on the phrase "فِيما تَرَكْتُ‌"

In the above verses, we read that the disbelievers on the threshold of death request to be returned so that they may perform righteous deeds "in that which they left behind."

Some believe that "فِيما تَرَكْتُ‌" here refers to the wealth left behind, which in common terminology is referred to as the "estate of the deceased" (Tarakah).

A Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام is a witness to this meaning, where he said: "Whoever withholds a qirat of Zakat is neither a believer nor a Muslim, and that is [the meaning of] the words of Allah Almighty: 'رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ‌'." [1]

While others hold a broader meaning for it and consider "ما تركت" (what I left) as a reference to all the righteous deeds they abandoned. That is: "O Lord! Return me so that I may compensate for the righteous deeds I failed to do." If the Hadith quoted above is of the type that explains a clear instance, it does not contradict this broad and comprehensive interpretation. Given that such individuals regret all the opportunities they lost and desire to compensate for all of them, the second interpretation seems more correct.

Furthermore, the expression "لعل" (perhaps) in the phrase "لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً" (perhaps I may do righteous deeds) might indicate that these polluted and deviated individuals are not fully certain of their own future state either. They know more or less that this regret and remorse are the result of the specific conditions they found themselves in at the threshold of death, and perhaps if they were to return, they would continue the same past path—and such is the reality.

[1] Al-Kafi, Thawab al-A'mal, and Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih (as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 552).

3- What does "كَلَّا" (Nay/Never) negate here?

"كَلَّا" is a word in the Arabic language used for prohibition, prevention, and invalidating the speech of the opponent; it is effectively the opposite of "ای" (Yes), which is for affirmation.

In response to the above question, some have said: "كَلَّا" negates the disbelievers' request to return to worldly life, meaning: the path of return is closed and is by no means possible.

Others have said that this word is to negate their claim when they say, "If we return to the world, we will compensate for the past." Allah says: This is a baseless and hollow claim, and if they were to return, the program would be the same as before.

At the same time, there is no obstacle to this word referring to the negation of both meanings.

It is also necessary to mention that although this request and answer are mentioned in the above verse only regarding the polytheists, it is certain that it is not exclusive to them. Rather, it is the request of all sinners, oppressors, and the polluted who, upon seeing their painful fate at the threshold of death, regret the past and request to return, but they are met with rejection.

4- What is the World of Barzakh?

What kind of world is the world of Barzakh? Where is it? And what is the evidence for proving such a world that lies between this world and the Hereafter?

Is Barzakh for everyone or for a specific group?

And finally, what is the state of the believers and the righteous, as well as the disbelievers and the wicked, therein?

These are questions that exist in this regard, and hints have been made to them in verses and narrations. It is necessary to address their answers as far as the scope of this book allows.

The word "Barzakh" originally means something that acts as a barrier between two things, and subsequently, anything that is placed between two matters is called "Barzakh." For this reason, the world that is situated between this world and the Hereafter is called "Barzakh."

The evidence for the existence of such a world—which is sometimes referred to as the "world of the grave" or the "world of spirits"—is through transmitted evidence (adillah naqliyyah). We have numerous verses in the Holy Quran, some of which are apparent (zuhur) and some explicit (sarahat) in this meaning.

The verse under discussion (وَ مِنْ وَرائِهِمْ بَرْزَخٌ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ‌) is apparent in the existence of such a world, even though some have wanted to introduce the word Barzakh in this verse as a barrier to returning to this world, saying the meaning of the verse is that behind man is a barrier preventing him from returning to this world. However, this meaning seems very unlikely, because the expression "إِلى‌ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ‌" (until the Day of Resurrection) is evidence that this Barzakh is situated between this world and the Hereafter, not between man and the world.

Among the verses that "explicitly" prove the existence of such a world are the verses related to the life of the martyrs, such as: وَ لا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْواتاً بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ‌: "Think not of those who are killed in the way of Allah as dead. Nay, they are alive, finding their sustenance in the presence of their Lord" (Surah Al-Imran, verse 169).

Here the address is to the Prophet, and in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 154, the address is to all believers, saying: وَ لا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْواتٌ بَلْ أَحْياءٌ وَ لكِنْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ‌.

Not only does the world of Barzakh exist for high-ranking believers such as martyrs, but regarding tyrannical disbelievers such as Pharaoh and his followers, the existence of Barzakh is explicitly mentioned in Surah Ghafir, verse 46:

النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها غُدُوًّا وَ عَشِيًّا وَ يَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذابِ‌: "The Fire; they are exposed to it morning and evening; and on the Day when the Hour is established [it will be said]: 'Cast the people of Pharaoh into the severest punishment.'"

Of course, other verses have also been mentioned for this subject, which do not reach the level of the above verses in explicitness and appearance. The only point that must be noted in the verses of Barzakh is that except for the verse under discussion, which states the issue of Barzakh generally, the rest of them present the issue in a specific form, for example, regarding "martyrs" or "the people of Pharaoh."

However, it is clear that neither the people of Pharaoh have a unique status—as there have been many like them in the world—nor the martyrs, for the Holy Quran has counted a group of the righteous and the elite as their equals. In Surah An-Nisa, verse 69, it places prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous alongside each other: فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَ الصِّدِّيقِينَ وَ الشُّهَداءِ وَ الصَّالِحِينَ‌.

Regarding whether Barzakh is universal or not, we have a discussion that will come at the end of this topic, God willing.

As for the narrations—in the famous books of the Shi'a and Sunnis, there are numerous narrations that speak with various and completely different expressions about the world of Barzakh, the world of the grave, the spirits, and in short, the world that lies between this world and the Hereafter:

1- In a famous Hadith found in the short sayings of Nahj al-Balagha, we read that Ali عليه السلام, upon returning from the Battle of Siffin, when he reached near Kufa by a cemetery located outside the gate, turned toward the graves and said:

"O inhabitants of lonely houses, desolate places, and dark graves! O people of the dust! O people of exile! O people of solitude! O people of loneliness! You are our preceding pioneers and we are your following successors. As for the houses, they have been inhabited; as for the wives, they have been married; and as for the wealth, it has been divided. This is the news we have; what is the news you have?"

Then he turned to his companions and said: "If they were permitted to speak, they would surely inform you that the best provision is piety (taqwa)." [1]

[1] Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 130.

It is clear that all these expressions cannot be taken as metaphor or allusion; rather, they all report the reality that man, after death, possesses a type of purgatorial life—he understands, perceives, and if permitted to speak, he would speak.

2- In another Hadith from Asbagh ibn Nubata from Ali عليه السلام, we read that one day the Imam left Kufa and came near the land of "Ghari" (Najaf) and passed it. Asbagh says: We reached him and saw him lying on the ground. Qanbar said: "O Commander of the Faithful, will you not allow me to spread my cloak under your feet?" He replied:

"No, this is a land where the dust of the believers is located, and this act of yours would be a disturbance to them."

Asbagh says: I said, "I understood what the dust of the believer is, but what does disturbing them mean?"

He replied: "O son of Nubata, if the veil were lifted from before your eyes, you would see the spirits of the believers in this highland in circles, visiting one another and conversing. Indeed, in this highland is the spirit of every believer, and in the Valley of Barahut is the soul of every disbeliever." [1]

3- In another Hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, we read: "The grave is either a garden from the gardens of Paradise or a pit from the pits of Hell!" [2]

[1] Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 243. [2] Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 553.

4- In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "Barzakh is the grave, and it is the reward and punishment between the world and the Hereafter... By Allah, we do not fear for you except for Barzakh!" [1]

5- In another Hadith narrated in the book Al-Kafi, after mentioning this sentence, we read that the narrator asked the Imam: "And what is Barzakh?" The Imam replied: "The grave from the time of death until the Day of Resurrection." [2]

6- In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that a person said to him: "A group narrates that the spirits of the believers after death are placed in the crops of green birds that fly around the Divine Throne?!" The Imam replied: "It is not so; the believer is more honorable to Allah than for Him to place his spirit in the crop of a bird; rather, they are in bodies like their [worldly] bodies." [3]

This Hadith refers to the "imaginal form" (qalib mithali) which resembles this physical body in some aspects but possesses a type of purgatorial immateriality.

7- Again, in another Hadith narrated in the book Al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, they asked him about the spirits of the believers. He said: "In chambers in Paradise, they eat from its food and drink from its drinks, and they say: 'Our Lord! Establish the Hour for us and fulfill what You promised us.'" [4]

[1] Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 553. [2] Ibid, p. 554. [3] Al-Kafi (as quoted in Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 268). [4] Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 269.

8- In another Hadith in the same book from the same great Imam, it is stated: "When one of the believers passes away, the spirits of the believers surround him and inquire about those who were in the world, whether they are alive or dead. If he says so-and-so has passed away but they do not see him present among them, they say: 'He must have fallen (meaning he has been placed in Hell!)'." [1]

It is evident that the "Paradise" and "Hell" intended in the above narrations are the purgatorial Paradise and Hell, not the world of Resurrection, as these two differ greatly.

In summary, the narrations in this field are very numerous and have been collected in various chapters, some of which we mention: - Numerous narrations that speak of the questioning, the pressure, and the punishment of the grave. - Narrations that discuss the contact of spirits with their families and observing their state. - Narrations that speak of the events of the Night of Ascension (Mi'raj) and the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله contact with the spirits of the prophets. - Narrations that say: the result of good or bad deeds that man performed in this world reaches him after death... and the like. [2]

[1] Ibid. [2] These narrations were collected by the late Sayyid Abdullah Shubbar in the book Tasliyat al-Fu'ad fi Bayan al-Mawt wa al-Ma'ad.

Barzakh and Communication with the World of Spirits—Although many who claim communication with the world of spirits are lying or are caught in imagination and fancy, according to research conducted, it has been proven that communication with the world of spirits is possible. This matter has been experienced by a group of knowledgeable people who have perceived realities through contact with spirits.

This matter is itself a clear proof for proving the world of Barzakh and its reality, and it shows that after the world of the material and the death of the body, and before the rising of the Hereafter, another world exists. [1] Likewise, the rational proofs we have for proving the immateriality of the soul and its survival after the destruction of the body are another proof for proving the world of Barzakh (reflect upon this).

A Depiction of the World of Barzakh

If we set aside the minor details, there is a consensus among Islamic scholars regarding the issue of Barzakh and the punishment and blessing in this world; the Shi'a and Sunni scholars are united in this matter, except for a negligible few.

The reason for this consensus is also clear, because the existence of the world of Barzakh and its blessings and punishments, as we said, is explicitly mentioned in the verses of the Holy Quran.

Regarding the martyrs, the Quran explicitly says: one should not imagine that they are dead; they are alive and receive blessings and sustenance near their Lord, and they are happy with what Allah has given them, and they even give glad tidings to those left behind that they have no fear or grief (Al-Imran, verse 169).

[1] For more explanation on the issue of spirit communication, refer to the book Return of Spirits and Communication with Spirits and the book The World After Death.

Not only is this group of righteous people blessed, but a group of the worst tyrants and criminals are also in punishment, as we previously mentioned regarding the punishment of the people of Pharaoh after death and before the rising of the Resurrection (Surah Ghafir, verse 46).

And the narrations, as we have known, are at the level of tawatur (successive transmission) in Islamic sources.

Therefore, the principle of the existence of the world of Barzakh is not a matter of debate; what is important is to know what the purgatorial life is like. Various depictions have been mentioned here, the clearest of which is that the human soul, after the end of life in this world, is placed in subtle bodies that are free from many of the accidents of this dense matter. Since it resembles this body in every respect, it is called the "imaginal form" (qalib mithali) or "imaginal body" (badan mithali), which is neither entirely immaterial nor purely material, but possesses a type of "purgatorial immateriality."

Some researchers have likened it to the state of the soul during sleep, in which state it is possible to truly enjoy observing blessings or be punished and pained by seeing horrific scenes, such that sometimes its reaction appears in this very body; upon seeing horrific dreams, one cries out, screams, writhes, and the body becomes drenched in sweat.

Some even believe that during sleep, the soul truly acts with the imaginal form, and even higher than that, they believe that strong souls can attain that same purgatorial immateriality even while awake—meaning they separate from the body and travel the world with this same imaginal form by their own will, or through magnetic sleep, and become aware of matters. [1]

[1] The late Allamah Majlisi, while stating this matter in Bihar al-Anwar, specifies that the comparison of the state of Barzakh to the state of vision and sleep has appeared in many reports. It is even possible that high, strong souls possess multiple imaginal bodies, and by this means, the narrations saying that the Imams are present near everyone at the time of death will not need [strained] justification or interpretation (Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 271).

Rather, some specify that the imaginal form is within the body of every human, but it separates at the time of death and the beginning of the purgatorial life, and sometimes in this material life of the world, the possibility of separation—as we said—is achieved for it.

Now, even if we do not accept all these characteristics for the imaginal form, the core of the matter cannot be denied, as it has been referred to in many narrations and there is no obstacle to it from the perspective of rational proof.

Furthermore, from what we have said, the answer to the objection raised by some becomes clear: they say that belief in an imaginal body necessitates belief in the issue of reincarnation (tanasukh), because reincarnation is nothing other than a single soul being transferred to multiple bodies.

The late Shaykh Baha'i has clearly stated the answer to this objection; he says: The reincarnation that all Muslims agree is false is that the soul, after the destruction of this body, returns to another body in this very world. However, the soul's attachment to the imaginal body in the "world of Barzakh" until the rising of the Resurrection—when it returns to the original bodies by the command of Allah—has no connection to reincarnation. If you see that we strongly deny reincarnation and declare its believers as disbelievers, it is because they believe in the pre-eternity of souls and their permanent transfer from one body to another, and they completely deny the physical resurrection in the other world. [1] And if, as some have said, the imaginal form is within this very material body, the answer to the issue of reincarnation becomes even clearer, because the soul has not transferred from its form to another form, but rather has abandoned some of its forms and continued purgatorial life with another.

[1] Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 277.

Another question that remains here is that it is understood from some verses of the Quran that a group of people do not have a world of Barzakh, as we read in verses 55 and 56 of Surah Ar-Rum: "A group of criminals, after the Resurrection is established, swear that they stayed but an hour in the world of Barzakh, but soon the knowledgeable believers say to them: 'You have stayed, by the command of Allah, for a long time until the Day of Resurrection, and now is the Day of Resurrection.'"

The answer to this objection has been given in several narrations as follows: People are of three groups: A group of pure believers, a group of pure disbelievers, and a group who are intermediate and weak (mustad'af). The world of Barzakh is specific to the first and second groups, but the third group passes through the world of Barzakh in a type of state of unawareness (for more information on these narrations, refer to Vol. 6 of Bihar al-Anwar, the discussion on the states of Barzakh and the grave).

Ayahs

فَإِذَا نُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَلَآ أَنسَابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَلَا يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (١٠١)
When the Trumpet is blown, there will be no ties between them on that day, nor will they ask [about] each other. (101)
فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (١٠٢)
Then those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales—it is they who are the felicitous. (102)
وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فِى جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدُونَ (١٠٣)
As for those whose deeds weigh light in the scales—they will be the ones who have ruined their souls, and they will remain in hell [forever]. (103)
تَلْفَحُ وُجُوهَهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَهُمْ فِيهَا كَـٰلِحُونَ (١٠٤)
The Fire will scorch their faces, while they snarl baring their teeth. (104)

Commentary

A Glimpse into the Punishment of the Evildoers

In the preceding verses, as we have seen, the discussion pertained to the world of Barzakh (the purgatory). Following that, the verses under discussion speak of the Resurrection and some of the states of the criminals in that world.

First, It says: "Then, when the Trumpet is blown, there will be no kinship between them that Day, nor will they ask of one another" (فَإِذا نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَلا أَنْسابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَ لا يَتَساءَلُونَ).

We know, according to the verses of the Quran, that the "Blowing of the Trumpet" occurs twice: once at the end of this world, after which all those in the heavens and the earth shall die and death will encompass the entire universe. After the second blowing, the resurrection of the dead begins, and humans return to a new life, prepared for reckoning and recompense.

"Nafkh al-Sur" literally means blowing into a horn or trumpet, but this expression has a specific interpretation and concept which, God willing, we will explain in detail under verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar.

In any case, the above verse refers to two phenomena of the Resurrection: one is the cessation of lineage and kinship. This is because the tribal and family ties that govern the system of life in this world often cause criminals to escape many punishments or seek help from relatives to solve their problems. However, on the Day of Resurrection, it is man and his deeds; no one can defend even his brother, child, or father, nor can anyone take another's punishment upon themselves.

The second point is that they will be so plunged into terror that, due to the intensity of the fear of the Divine reckoning and punishment, they will not ask about one another's condition at all. That day is a day when a mother becomes heedless of her nursing infant, and a brother forgets his brother; people appear as if they are drunk, yet they are not drunk—the punishment of Allah is severe! As we read at the beginning of Surah Al-Hajj: "On the Day you see it, every nursing mother will be distracted from that [child] she was nursing, and every pregnant woman will abort her pregnancy, and you will see the people [appearing] intoxicated while they are not intoxicated; but the punishment of Allah is severe" (يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَها تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّا أَرْضَعَتْ وَ تَضَعُ كُلُّ ذاتِ حَمْلٍ حَمْلَها وَ تَرَى النَّاسَ سُكارى‌ وَ ما هُمْ بِسُكارى‌ وَ لكِنَّ عَذابَ اللَّهِ شَدِيدٌ).

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the phrase "nor will they ask of one another" (وَ لا يَتَساءَلُونَ) that it means they will not request help from one another, for they know that such a request is entirely useless and ineffective.

Some commentators have also said that the negation of "asking" means they will not ask about lineage, serving as an emphasis on the phrase "there will be no kinship between them" (فَلا أَنْسابَ بَيْنَهُمْ).

Of course, the first interpretation seems the clearest, although there is no contradiction between them, and the phrase may refer to all these meanings.

Here, a well-known question arises in the words of the commentators: some verses of the Quran clearly indicate that on the Day of Resurrection, people will ask of one another. For instance, verse 27 of Surah As-Saffat says regarding the criminals when they stand at the threshold of Hell: "And they will approach one another, questioning each other" (وَ أَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ)—meaning they turn to one another and ask (reproachful) questions.

In the same Surah, verse 50 speaks of the people of Paradise, that when they are settled in Paradise, they turn toward one another and ask (about the companions they had in the world who went to Hell due to deviating from the path of truth): "Then they will approach one another, questioning each other" (فأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ).

A similar meaning appears in verse 25 of Surah At-Tur. Now the question is: how are these verses compatible with the verse under discussion which says they will not ask of one another on the Day of Resurrection?

A little reflection on the context of the verses cited above clarifies the answer. The verses affirming that they ask one another pertain to the time after being settled in Paradise or at the threshold of Hell, whereas the negation of asking one another pertains to the initial stages of the Resurrection, where the horror, agitation, and terror are so overwhelming that they completely forget one another.

In other words, the Resurrection has various "stations" (mawaqif), and in each station, there is a specific program. Sometimes, failing to notice the multiplicity of these stations leads to questions such as the one mentioned above.

After the occurrence of the Resurrection, the first issue is the weighing of deeds with the specific "Scale" (Mizan) designated for this purpose on that Day. A group will have heavy deeds that weigh down the scale of deeds. Regarding this group, He says: "Then those whose scales are heavy—it is they who are the successful" (فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ).

"Mawazin" is the plural of "Mizan," meaning a means of measuring weight. As we have previously explained in detail, the "Scale" for weighing deeds is not a physical scale with two pans used for measuring the weight of objects; rather, it refers to a suitable means by which the value of human deeds is measured. In other words, "Mizan" has a broad concept that includes all means of measurement. As understood from various narrations, the scale for measuring human deeds—and even humans themselves—on that Day are the great leaders and exemplary human beings.

In a narration, we read: "Indeed, the Commander of the Faithful and the Imams from his progeny are the Scales" (ان امير المؤمنين و الأئمه من ذريته هم الموازين) [1]. Therefore, humans and their deeds are compared with the great Prophets and their successors, and in this comparison, it becomes clear to what extent they resemble them.

Through this method, "individuals" and "deeds" that are weighty are distinguished from those that are weightless, the heavy from the hollow, the valuable from the worthless, and the substantial from the insubstantial.

Furthermore, the reason for mentioning "Mawazin" in the plural becomes clear, as the great leaders who serve as the scales of measurement are multiple.

It is also possible that each of the Prophets, Imams, and special servants of Allah, by virtue of their life circumstances, were models in one or several directions, and each shone more prominently in a certain aspect. Thus, each will be a scale of measurement in that particular area.

"But those whose scales are light (or weightless) due to a lack of faith and righteous deeds are those who have lost their own souls and incurred loss, and they will abide in Hell forever" (وَ مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ فِي جَهَنَّمَ خالِدُونَ).

The expression "lost their own souls" (خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ) is a subtle reference to the reality that they lost their greatest capital—their very existence—in the marketplace of this world without gaining anything valuable in return.

The next verse describes a part of their painful punishments: "The fire will sear their faces" (تَلْفَحُ وُجُوهَهُمُ النَّارُ). "And they will have therein a grimace, with displaced lips" (وَ هُمْ فِيها كالِحُونَ).

"Talfah" is derived from "lafh," which originally means a sword strike. Since flames of fire, intense sunlight, or scorching winds strike a person's face like a sword, it is used metaphorically in this sense.

"Kalih" is from "kuluuh," meaning to be morose or to have a contorted face. Many commentators have interpreted it to mean that due to the flames of fire, the skin of their faces will be so shriveled and drawn back that their lips will remain parted [1].

Key Points

1. The Day When Lineage Loses Its Effect

The concepts that govern the material life of humans in this world are mostly transformed in the other world. Among these is the issue of tribal and family connections, which are often problem-solvers in this life and sometimes form a system that dominates other social systems.

However, given that the values of life in the hereafter are aligned with faith and righteous deeds, the matter of being attributed to a certain person, clan, or tribe can have no place. Here, members of a family help one another and save each other from troubles, but in the Resurrection, it is not so. There, there is no news of abundant wealth, nor can children do anything: "The Day when neither wealth nor children will benefit, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart" (يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَ لا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ) [2].

Even if this lineage reaches the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) himself, it is still subject to this same rule. For this reason, we read in the history of the lives of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the noble Imams (peace be upon them) that they officially rejected some of the closest members of the Bani Hashim due to their lack of faith or deviation from the authentic path of Islam, expressing loathing and dissociation from them.

Although it is narrated in a tradition from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) that he said: "Every hasab [1] and nasab (lineage) will be severed on the Day of Resurrection except my hasab and nasab" [2], as the late Allamah Tabataba'i (may Allah be pleased with him) noted in Al-Mizan, it appears this is the same tradition that a group of Sunni traditionists have narrated in their books, sometimes from Abdullah ibn Umar, sometimes from Umar ibn al-Khattab himself, and sometimes from other companions from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family).

However, the outward meaning of the verse under discussion is general and speaks of the severance of all lineages in the Resurrection. The principles derived from the Quran and the conduct of the Prophet toward faithless deviants indicate that there is no difference between humans in this regard.

Thus, in a narration that the author of Al-Manaqib, Ibn Shahr Ashub, narrated from Tawus (al-Yamani) from Imam Zayn al-Abidin (peace be upon him), we read: "Allah created Paradise for whoever obeys Him and does good, even if he be an Abyssinian slave; and He created the Fire for whoever disobeys Him, even if he be a child of the Quraysh" [1]. Of course, what has been said does not contradict the special respect for the Sayyids and the pious descendants of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), for this respect is itself a respect for the person of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and Islam. The narrations regarding their virtue and status are also apparently directed toward this meaning.

2. The Stirring Story of Asma'i

It is appropriate here to bring a story that Al-Ghazali narrated in the book Bahr al-Mahabbah from Asma'i, which serves as a witness to the previous words and contains subtle points:

Asma'i says: "I was in Mecca on a moonlit night. While I was circumambulating the House of Allah, a beautiful and sorrowful voice caressed my ears. I searched for the owner of the voice until my eyes fell upon a beautiful, well-statured youth upon whom the marks of goodness were apparent. He had his hands clinging to the curtain of the Kaaba and was supplicating thus:

'O my Master and my Lord! The eyes have slept and the stars have set, and You are the King, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer. Neither slumber nor sleep overtakes You. The kings have locked their doors and stationed their guards and chamberlains over them, and every lover has secluded himself with his beloved. But Your door is open to the seekers. Here I am, a seeker at Your door, a sinful pauper, a mistaken wretch. I have come to You hoping for Your mercy, O Merciful! And that You look upon me with Your grace, O Generous!'"

Then he began reciting these verses: "O You who answers the prayer of the distressed in the darkness, O Remover of grief, affliction, and sickness. Your guests have slept around the House and then awakened, but the eye of Your generosity, O Sustainer, never sleeps. If Your generosity is only hoped for by those of nobility, then who shall bestow blessings upon the sinners? ... Grant me by Your generosity the favor of pardon out of nobility, O You to whom the creation points in the Sanctuary."

Then he raised his head toward the sky and continued: "My God, my Master, and my Lord! If I have obeyed You through my knowledge and recognition, then to You belongs the praise and the favor over me. And if I have disobeyed You through my ignorance, then Your proof is established against me..." Once again he raised his head to the sky and said loudly: "O my God, my Master, and my Lord! The world is not pleasant except with Your remembrance, and the hereafter is not pleasant except with Your pardon, and the days are not pleasant except with Your obedience, and the hearts are not pleasant except with Your love, and the blessings are not pleasant except with Your forgiveness!"

Asma'i says: "The youth continued and recited other stirring and very attractive verses of the same theme, and he recited and recited until he lost consciousness and fell to the ground. I approached him and stared at his face (the moonlight was falling on his face). I looked closely and suddenly realized he was Zayn al-Abidin Ali ibn al-Husayn, Imam al-Sajjad (peace be upon him).

I took his head in my lap and wept bitterly for his state. A drop of my tear fell on his face; he regained consciousness, opened his eyes, and said: 'Who is it that has distracted me from the remembrance of my Master?!' I said, 'I am Asma'i, O my master and lord. What is this weeping and what is this restlessness? You are from the household of Prophethood and the mine of the Message. Has not the Verse of Purification been revealed regarding you? Has not Allah said about you: "Allah only intends to remove from you the impurity [of sin], O people of the [Prophet's] household, and to purify you with [thorough] purification"?'

The Imam stood up, sat down, and said: 'O Asma'i! Far from it, far from it! Allah created Paradise for the obedient, even if he be an Abyssinian slave, and He created the Fire for the disobedient, even if he be a great person of the Quraysh. Have you not read the Quran and heard this word of Allah where He says: "Then, when the Trumpet is blown, there will be no kinship between them that Day, nor will they ask of one another..."?

When the Trumpet is blown and the Resurrection occurs, lineage will be of no use; rather, the scale for weighing deeds must be heavy.' Asma'i says: 'When I saw him in such a state, I left him to himself and moved away'" [1].

3. The Proportionality of Punishment and Sin

We have previously noted that Divine punishment in the Resurrection, and even in this world, is proportional to the crimes committed. It is not the case that every type of punishment is applied to every type of criminal. In the above verses, the severe burning of faces by the flames of Hellfire—to the point that the faces are contorted and the lips remain parted—is mentioned as a punishment for those whose "scales are light," the worthless and faithless. Considering that they are often those who contort their faces upon hearing the Divine verses, sometimes giving a mocking smile and sitting in derision and ridicule, the proportionality of this punishment to their deeds becomes clear.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 7, p. 251 (New Edition). [1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, and Tafsir al-Mizan under the verses discussed. [2]: Ash-Shu'ara, 89. [1]: Hasab linguistically refers to the honors possessed by one's ancestors, fathers, and grandfathers, and sometimes it refers to a person's own character and traits, but here the first meaning is intended (refer to Lisan al-Arab, root h-s-b). [2]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed. [1]: Manaqib Ibn Shahr Ashub (as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 564). [1]: Bahr al-Mahabbah by Al-Ghazali, pp. 41 to 44 (with some abridgment).

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَكُنْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَكُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ (١٠٥)
‘Was it not that My signs were recited to you but you would deny them?’ (105)
قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا غَلَبَتْ عَلَيْنَا شِقْوَتُنَا وَكُنَّا قَوْمًا ضَآلِّينَ (١٠٦)
They will say, ‘Our Lord! Our wretchedness overcame us, and we were an astray lot. (106)
رَبَّنَآ أَخْرِجْنَا مِنْهَا فَإِنْ عُدْنَا فَإِنَّا ظَـٰلِمُونَ (١٠٧)
Our Lord! Bring us out of this! Then, if we revert [to our previous conduct], we will indeed be wrongdoers.’ (107)
قَالَ ٱخْسَـُٔوا۟ فِيهَا وَلَا تُكَلِّمُونِ (١٠٨)
He will say, ‘Begone in it, and do not speak to Me! (108)
إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْ عِبَادِى يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (١٠٩)
Indeed there was a part of My servants who would say, ‘‘Our Lord! We have believed. So forgive us, and have mercy on us, and You are the best of the merciful.’’ (109)
فَٱتَّخَذْتُمُوهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا حَتَّىٰٓ أَنسَوْكُمْ ذِكْرِى وَكُنتُم مِّنْهُمْ تَضْحَكُونَ (١١٠)
But you took them by ridicule until they made you forget My remembrance, and you used to laugh at them. (110)
إِنِّى جَزَيْتُهُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ بِمَا صَبَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (١١١)
Indeed I have rewarded them today for their patience. They are indeed the triumphant.’ (111)

Commentary

Do Not Speak to Me!

In the preceding verses, the discussion focused on the painful punishment of the inhabitants of Hell. Following those verses, the current passage recounts a portion of the Almighty’s dialogue with them. First, Allah addresses them with this rebuking speech, saying: "Were My verses not recited to you, and you used to deny them?" (أَ لَمْ تَكُنْ آياتِي تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ فَكُنْتُمْ بِها تُكَذِّبُونَ‌) [^1]. Did I not send enough clear verses and proofs through My prophets to you? Did I not complete the proof (Hujjah) against you, while you continuously chose the path of denial and rejection?

Specifically, considering the words "تتلى" (are recited) and "تكذبون" (you deny), both of which are in the present continuous tense (Mudari') indicating persistence, it becomes clear that the recitation of Divine verses to them was continuous, just as their denial in the face of these verses was persistent!

In response to this question, they confess and "say: Yes, it is so, O our Lord! But our wretchedness and misery overcame us, and we were a people astray" (قالُوا رَبَّنا غَلَبَتْ عَلَيْنا شِقْوەُنا وَ كُنَّا قَوْماً ضالِّينَ‌).

[^1]: In reality, this sentence contains an omitted part, and its implied meaning is: "Allah Almighty says: Were [My verses] not..."

"Shiqwah" and "Shaqawah" (wretchedness) are the opposite of "Sa'adah" (felicity/happiness). It means the availability of the causes of distress, punishment, and calamity. In other words, it is an evil or affliction that overtakes a person, whereas felicity means the availability of the causes of blessing and goodness. In any case, both (wretchedness and felicity) are nothing but the result of our own actions, words, and intentions. The belief that felicity and wretchedness are innate matters born with a person is nothing more than a delusion that contradicts the call of all prophets and the efforts of all guides and teachers of humanity. It is a delusion created to escape the burden of responsibility and to justify wrongdoings and crimes, or to interpret instances of ignorance.

Based on this, the sinners in Hell explicitly confess that the proof was completed by Allah, but we provided the means of our own misery by our own hands, and we admit that we were a people astray.

Perhaps with these confessions, they hope to attract the mercy of the Lord; therefore, they immediately add: "Our Lord! Bring us out of this [Fire]" and return us to the world so that we may perform righteous deeds (رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْها). "If we ever return [to our former ways], then we are indeed wrongdoers" and will not be worthy of Your forgiveness (فَإِنْ عُدْنا فَإِنَّا ظالِمُونَ‌).

They say this as if they are unaware of the reality that the Hereafter is the abode of recompense, not action, and that returning to the world is no longer possible.

For this reason, He answers them with absolute decisiveness: "Allah says: Be driven away into it and remain therein, be silent and do not speak to Me!" (قالَ اخْسَؤُا فِيها وَ لا تُكَلِّمُونِ‌).

The word "Ikhsa'u," which is in the imperative form, is typically used to drive away a dog. When used regarding a human, it signifies their degradation and their worthiness of punishment.

Then, He explains the reason for this statement: "Have you forgotten that a group of My special servants used to say: Our Lord, we have believed, so forgive us and have mercy on us, and You are the best of the merciful"?! (إِنَّهُ كانَ فَرِيقٌ مِنْ عِبادِي يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا آمَنَّا فَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَ ارْحَمْنا وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّاحِمِينَ‌).

"But you took them as a laughingstock and persisted in this so much that mocking them made you completely forget My remembrance" (فَاتَّخَذْتُمُوهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا حَتَّى أَنْسَوْكُمْ ذِكْرِي‌). "And you used to laugh at them," sneering at their words, beliefs, behavior, and deeds (وَ كُنْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ تَضْحَكُونَ‌).

"But today, because of their patience, perseverance, and steadfastness in the face of all that mockery and their lack of wavering in their Divine programs, I have rewarded them; they are the triumphant and the successful" (إِنِّي جَزَيْتُهُمُ الْيَوْمَ بِما صَبَرُوا أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ‌). But as for you... today you are trapped in the worst of states and the most painful of punishments, and no one comes to your aid; and it must be so, for you are deserving of this penalty.

Thus, in these last four verses, the primary cause of the wretchedness of the people of Hell and the cause of the victory and salvation of the people of Paradise are explicitly stated.

The first group, who provided the causes of misery and misguidance by their own hands, fell into a fate where they are not even worthy of being addressed as humans because they mocked the companions of Truth and belittled their pure beliefs. Indeed, those who belittled the believers must suffer the worst of humiliations.

As for the second group, because of their patience and steadfastness against arrogant, self-satisfied, and illogical enemies, and their persistence in following the path of "Allah," they achieved the greatest victory in the presence of God.

Ayahs

قَـٰلَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ (١١٢)
He will say, ‘How many years did you remain on earth?’ (112)
قَالُوا۟ لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ فَسْـَٔلِ ٱلْعَآدِّينَ (١١٣)
They will say, ‘We remained for a day, or part of a day; yet ask those who keep the count.’ (113)
قَـٰلَ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا ۖ لَّوْ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١١٤)
He will say, ‘You only remained a little; if only you had known. (114)
أَفَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّمَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ عَبَثًا وَأَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْنَا لَا تُرْجَعُونَ (١١٥)
Did you suppose that We created you aimlessly, and that you will not be brought back to Us?’ (115)
فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْكَرِيمِ (١١٦)
So exalted is Allah, the True Sovereign, there is no god except Him, the Lord of the Noble Throne. (116)

Commentary

The Shortness of the Life of This World

Since the preceding verses mentioned a portion of the painful punishment of the people of Hell, this section of verses follows up by presenting another type of their psychological punishment, which manifests as Divine rebukes:

First, it says: "On that day, Allah will address them, saying: 'How many years did you stay and live on the earth?'" (قالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ).

The word "earth" (ard) in this verse, as well as the context provided in the subsequent verses, indicates that the question pertains to the duration of their lives in the world in comparison to the days of the Hereafter.

The view held by a group of commentators—that the question refers to the duration of their stay in the world of Barzakh (the Isthmus)—seems unlikely, even though some brief evidence for it can be found in certain other verses. [^1]

However, in this comparison, they perceive the life of the world to be so short that "in response, they say: 'We stayed for only a day, or even less than that, the measure of part of a day'" (قالُوا لَبِثْننا يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ). In reality, long lifetimes in the world are like fleeting moments in

[^1]: In Surah Ar-Rum, verses 55 and 56, we read: "And on the Day the Hour is established, the criminals will swear they remained but an hour. Thus they were deluded. But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, 'You remained the extent of Allah's decree until the Day of Resurrection, and this is the Day of Resurrection, but you did not know.'" This verse indicates that the question and answer relate to the stay in Barzakh. If we take this as a contextual clue for the verses under discussion, their meaning would also be the stay in Barzakh. However, as we mentioned, the internal evidence in the verses under discussion is stronger and indicates that the question and answer pertain to the stay in the world.

comparison to the life of the Hereafter, where both its blessings are eternal and its punishments are limitless.

Then, to emphasize their statement, or perhaps to provide a more accurate answer, they plead: O Allah! "Ask those who can count accurately and can well distinguish numbers in comparison to one another" (فَسْئَلِ الْعادِّينَ).

It is possible that "those who count" ('addin) refers to the angels who have precisely kept the record of human lifespans and their deeds, for they know this account better and more accurately than anyone else.

It is here that Allah, by way of reproach and rebuke, "says to them: 'Yes, you stayed but a little while in the world, if only you had known'" (قالَ إِنْ لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا لَوْ أَنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ). In fact, on the Day of Resurrection, they realize the reality that the lifespan of the world is nothing more than a day or an hour compared to the lifespan of the Hereafter. But while they were in this world, such veils of negligence and pride had fallen over their hearts and minds that they imagined the world to be eternal and the Hereafter to be a mere fantasy or a deferred promise! Therefore, Allah says: Yes, if you had the awareness of this reality that you realized on the Day of Judgment, you would have become acquainted with it while still in the world. [^1]

In the next verse, the discussion enters from another path—a very effective and instructive path to awaken this group—and says: "Did you then think that We created you in vain,

[^1]: According to what we mentioned above, the word law (if) in the verse is "conditional," and a clause is implied, making the total meaning: "If you had known, you would have known that you stayed but a little while." However, some commentators have said the word law here is in the sense of layta (if only/I wish), thus the sentence means: "I wish you had known this matter in the world."

and that you would not be returned to Us?!" (أَ فَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّما خَلَقْناكُمْ عَبَثاً وَ أَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْنا لا تُرْجَعُونَ).

This short and meaningful sentence expresses one of the most vivid proofs for the Resurrection and the reckoning and recompense of deeds. That is, if there were truly no Resurrection or Hereafter, the life of the world would be futile and vain. For the life of this world, with all the difficulties it possesses and all the systems, preliminaries, and programs that Allah has arranged for it, would be utterly hollow and meaningless if it were merely for these few days, as will be explained in the Key Points.

And since this statement—that creation is not in vain—is a significant claim requiring a firm proof, the next verse adds: "Allah, the Sovereign, the Truth, is far above that; there is no deity except Him, the Lord of the Noble Throne; He is exalted above having created the universe in vain and without purpose" (فَتَعالَى اللَّهُ الْمَلِكُ الْحَقُّ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْكَرِيمِ). In reality, one who performs a hollow and aimless act is either ignorant and unaware, or weak and incapable, or is inherently a false and futile being. But Allah, who encompasses all attributes of perfection (اللَّهُ)...

Allah, who is the Sovereign and Owner of the entire universe (الْمَلِكُ)...

And Allah, who is the Truth and from whom nothing but truth proceeds (الْحَقُّ)... how is it possible for His creation to be vain and aimless?

And if it were imagined that someone could prevent Him from reaching His goal, He negates this with the phrase لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ (there is no god but Him). By emphasizing the Lordship of Allah (رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْكَرِيمِ), which carries the meaning of the "Reforming Owner," He makes the purposeful nature of the universe even clearer.

In summary, in this verse, in addition to mentioning the word "Allah"—which itself is a summary reference to all the perfect attributes of God—four attributes are explicitly relied upon: the Ownership and Sovereignty of God, then the Truth of His existence, the non-existence of any partner for Him, and finally the station of Lordship (Rububiyyah). All of these are evidence that He does not perform an aimless act and has not created the world and human beings in vain.

The word "Throne" ('Arsh), as we have previously pointed out, refers to the totality of the universe, which is considered to be under the governance of Allah (for 'Arsh literally means high-pillared thrones, especially the throne of government of rulers, and this expression is a metaphor for the realm of the Lord's governance). For more explanation regarding the meaning of 'Arsh in the Holy Quran, please refer to Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 204 onwards (under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf).

As for the "Throne" being described as "Noble" (Karim), it is because the word Karim originally means noble, highly beneficial, and good. Since the Throne of the Lord possesses these qualities, it is described as Karim.

It is also necessary to mention that the description "Noble" is not exclusive to conscious beings like Allah or humans; rather, it is also applied to other things in the Arabic language. As we read in Surah Al-Hajj, under verse 50, regarding the righteous believers: "For them is forgiveness and a noble provision (rizqun karim)"—meaning highly valuable and blessed. As Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: this attribute is not used for small and insignificant goodness, but is reserved for cases where there is goodness and virtue of great importance.

Key Point: Death is Not the End Point of Life

We said that among the proofs presented in the discussion of the Hereafter to establish the existence of another world is the "issue of studying the system of this world." In other words, studying this "first creation" (nash'ah ula) testifies that the "next creation" (nash'ah ukhra) follows it.

Here, we deem it necessary to provide more explanation in this regard:

On one hand, we see that the world of creation, both in terms of grandeur and order, is extraordinarily vast, magnificent, and astonishing. The secrets of this world are so numerous that great scientists admit that all human knowledge compared to it is like a small page from a massive book; rather, all that we know of this world is in reality just the alphabet of this book.

Each of the massive galaxies of this universe contains several billion stars, and the number of galaxies and the distances between them are so immense that even calculating it at the speed of light—which travels 300,000 kilometers in one second—is terrifying.

The order and precision used in the structure of the smallest unit of this universe are the same as the order and precision seen in the structure of its largest units.

And man, in the midst of this, is at least the most perfect being we know and the highest product of this universe—as far as we know. These are all on one side.

On the other hand, we see that this highest known product of the universe, namely man, grows up amidst what distresses and great difficulties during his short life?

He has not yet left childhood behind with all its pains and problems and taken a fresh breath when the tumultuous period of youth arrives with its intense and crushing storms.

And before he has even firmed his footing in the season of youth, the period of senescence and old age appears before him with its pitiable state.

Is it believable that the goal of this great and massive apparatus, and this wonder of creation named man, is just this: to come into this world for a few days, pass through these three stages with their pains and difficulties, consume some food, wear some clothes, sleep and wake up, and then be annihilated and everything come to an end?

If it were truly so, would creation not be futile and vain? Would any rational being arrange all these massive systems for such a small goal?! Suppose the human species remains in this world for millions of years, and generations come and go one after another, and material sciences progress so much that they provide the best nutrition, clothing, housing, and the highest degree of welfare for mankind; but does this eating, drinking, dressing, sleeping, and waking up have the value for which all these systems were established?

Therefore, the study of this magnificent world is alone proof that it is a preliminary for a wider, more expansive, eternal, and everlasting world. Only the existence of such a world can give meaning to our lives and rescue it from nothingness and futility.

For this reason, it is not surprising that materialistic philosophers who do not believe in the Resurrection and the other world consider this universe aimless and hollow. Truly, if we also did not believe in such a world, we would join them in that sentiment. This is why we say that if death were the end point, the creation of the world would be in vain. Thus, we read in verse 62 of Surah Al-Waqi'ah: "And you have already known the first creation, so why do you not take heed?"—meaning, you who have seen this world (the first creation), why do you not reflect and believe in the world that follows it?!

Ayahs

وَمَن يَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ لَا بُرْهَـٰنَ لَهُۥ بِهِۦ فَإِنَّمَا حِسَابُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (١١٧)
Whoever invokes besides Allah another god of which he has no proof, his reckoning will indeed rest with his Lord. Indeed the faithless will not be felicitous. (117)
وَقُل رَّبِّ ٱغْفِرْ وَٱرْحَمْ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (١١٨)
Say, ‘My Lord, forgive and have mercy, and You are the best of the merciful.’ (118)

Commentary

The Successful and the Unsuccessful
