Since the previous verses discussed the issue of "the Resurrection" and emphasized the attributes of the Lord, the first verse under discussion points to Monotheism (Tawhid) and the negation of any form of polytheism, thereby completing the discussion of "the Origin and the Resurrection." It states: "And whoever invokes besides Allah another deity—and certainly he will have no proof for his claim—his account is only with his Lord" (وَ مَنْ يَدْعُ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ لا بُرْهانَ لَهُ بِهِ فَإِنَّما حِسابُهُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ‌) [1].

[1]: Some commentators consider the phrase "فَإِنَّما حِسابُهُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ‌" to be the recompense (jaza) for the condition in the sentence "مَنْ يَدْعُ مَعَ اللَّهِ‌" and regard the phrase "لا بُرْهانَ لَهُ بِهِ‌" as a parenthetical clause between the condition and the recompense, the result of which is to emphasize the ultimate goal of the sentence. However, others consider "لا بُرْهانَ لَهُ بِهِ‌" to be the recompense for the condition and view "فَإِنَّما حِسابُهُ‌..." as a derivative (tafri'). Yet, this possibility does not align with the established rules of Arabic literature, because according to the rule, the recompense in such cases must be preceded by "fa" (ف), and it should have been said "فلا برهان له." Some have also suggested that this phrase might be an adjective (sifah) or a state (hal), but the first possibility seems closest to the truth, although there is not much difference in terms of meaning.

Indeed, the polytheists rely solely on claims, and their arguments—such as blind imitation of their ancestors and similar superstitions—are vain and baseless matters. They deny the Resurrection despite its clear proofs, yet they accept polytheism without having any proof whatsoever. It is certain that Allah will settle the account of such individuals who have trampled upon the command of reason and have consciously wandered into the wilderness of disbelief and polytheism.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed, the disbelievers will not succeed" (إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الْكافِرُونَ‌), and the outcome of their deeds in this Divine reckoning is clear.

How remarkable it is that this Surah began with "قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ‌" (The believers have certainly succeeded) and its discussions conclude with "لا يُفْلِحُ الْكافِرُونَ‌" (The disbelievers will not succeed). This is the perspective of the lives of the believers and the disbelievers from beginning to end.

In the final verse of this noble Surah, as a general conclusion, the address is directed toward the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "And say: My Lord! Forgive and have mercy, and You are the best of those who show mercy" (وَ قُلْ رَبِّ اغْفِرْ وَ ارْحَمْ وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّاحِمِينَ‌). Now that a group is wandering in the wilderness of polytheism and a group is entangled in oppression and tyranny, entrust yourself to Allah, seek refuge in His grace and mercy, and ask Him for forgiveness and pardon.

And it is certain that this command is for all believers, even though the addressee is the person of the Prophet.

It is narrated in a tradition that: "The beginning of this Surah and its end are from the treasures of the Throne of Allah; whoever acts upon its first three verses and takes heed from its final four verses will be among the people of salvation, success, and prosperity" [1]. It is not unlikely that the "first three verses" of this Surah refer to the three verses that come after the sentence "قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ‌," one of which calls for humility in prayer, another for avoiding all vain and useless acts, and the third for Zakat—one being the human's relationship with Allah, another with the creation, and the third with oneself. The "final four verses" refer to verse 115 onwards, which discusses the creation not being in vain, the issue of the Resurrection, then Monotheism, and then total devotion to Allah and turning toward the Lord.

O Allah! By the right of the believers whose success You promised in this Surah—at the forefront of whom are the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام—place us in the ranks of this group and write the decree of success and prosperity in our name. O Allah! Encompass us in Your forgiveness and mercy, for You are the "Best of those who show mercy."

O Lord! Make the end of all our affairs good and protect us from every deviation and slip in the places of stumbling. Indeed, You are Powerful over all things (إِنَّكَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

End of Surah al-Mu'minun, the night of the 25th of Muharram al-Haram, 1403, corresponding to 21/8/1361 (November 12, 1982).

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, under the verses discussed.
